abstract_text: at 10 am on may 19, 1986, employees #1 through #3 were using water and acetone to clean a simpson muller-mixer. the mixer contained less than 10 pounds of residue from a magnesium-tefron-fluorel-acetone mixture. they were located in the process building at a remote location. a flash fire occurred, apparently when flammable vapors were ignited by static electricity. the employee's shift had started at 7 am. at 8 am, in accordance with company policy, they began the shut-down process because of high winds and 20% humidity. at 8:30 they were ordered to stopthe batch process and clean up. there was no monitoring of flammable vapors at the process building. combustible material was used for the construction of the employee's work platform and for the walls of the process building. the employees were given no formal training on the hazards of the chemicals they were using. employee #1 died 29 days later and employee #2 died 5 days later. they were burned over 88% of their bodies, especially the face, upper trunk, and extremities, and experienced renal failure and edema of the lungs. employee #3 also experienced extensive burns but survived, and was released from the hospital 2 months later on an outpatient basis.

at about 2:10 p.m. on march 11, 1990, employees #1, #2, and #3 had been using electric skilsaws to cut and remove a damaged interior vapor barrier from a 55,000 barrel crude oil tank. they had just returned from a break, during which time the coppus ventilator had been turned off. temperatures reached the mid-80s during the day, increasing the vapor concentration within the tank. an explosion fire occurred in which employees #1, #2, and #3 suffered second- and third-degree burns, smoke inhalation, and concussions. all three died. the model #5125 skilsaws were not approved for use in a class i atmosphere. the owner of the tank tested the atmosphere only once per day for percent lower exposure limit and oxygen. the accident was the result of using unapproved (for the location) equipment in a potentially flammable atmosphere.

a maintenance crew of three electricians was replacing a 480-volt, 400-ampere main disconnect box in an electrical control room. the electric power for the disconnect was restored after the box was plugged into the bus duct riser. two of the electricians were attaching the conductors to the lugs inside the box. a bolton one of the lugs sheared off, and a piece of the bolt dropped to the bottom of the disconnect box. one of them tried to retrieve the piece and contacted an energized part within the enclosure. this caused an electrical fault, and the ensuing electric arc burned the employee on his face and right arm. he was hospitalized for his injuries. the second electrician sustained burns over most of his face.

employee #1 was throwing a tie down chain over a log truck bed loaded with logs. one of the top logs rolled off the truck bed, striking him on his head. employee #1 was transported to a local hospital, where he later died.

employee #1 was on top of a fish feed storage tank without fall protection whenhe was struck by the boom of the tailer's auger that he was using to fill the tank with feed. he fell 25 feet to the ground. employee #1 died on october 25, 199 3, from the injuries he received.

employee #1, a plant manager at the site, called employee #2, who worked for a separate tire servicing company, to the site to repair or replace a flat tire on a loader/dozer. employee #2 removed the old tire, inspected the rim and wheel, installed the new tire, put the rim on, and began to inflate the tire. employees #1 and #2, as well as the vehicle operator, were standing around talking when the tire exploded off the rim. employee #2 was at the edge of the tire's trajectory and was just grazed by it. employee #1 was hit full force by the tire and was killed instantly. no tire cage or restraining device was used, nor were the employees kept out of the trajectory range.

at approximately 11:55 a.m. on march 29, 1994, employee #1 was walking on a structural steel system approximately 35 ft above the ground. he was not using any fall protection and fell to the ground, fracturing his jaw. employee #1 was hospitalized.

a telephone installer was filling out a time sheet. the 41-year-old employee complained of tightness in chest, then fell backwards in his chair onto the floor. a coworker performed cardio-pulmonary resuscitation until emergency medical services arrived and transported the injured employee to a hospital. the employee died, apparently from a heart attack, at 8:35 am.

employee #1 was installing particleboard sheathing on the roof of a three-story condominium. the pitched portion of the roof was covered with frost, and employee #1 slipped when he stepped onto it. he slid off the roof and fell 30 ft 6 in. to the ground. employee #1 sustained injuries that required hospitalization.

on august 9, 1997, employee #1 was being trained to operate a rubber rolling mill. while cutting and mixing the rubber from the mill roll, his glove became stuck to the rubber as he tried to balance himself. he could not free his hand fromthe glove nor his glove from the rubber. he activated the emergency stop wire, but his hand went into the pinch point and was amputated approximately 2 in. above his wrist bone.

at 8:30 a.m. on august 17, 1987, employee #1 was placing and removing metal part s on a niagara mechanical press brake, serial #46950. the brake was tripped by a foot pedal and equipped with light curtains. employee #1 was taking a sheet metal part out of the die when the brake double-tripped and amputated three fingers on her right hand. employee #1 was hospitalized.

employee #1, who feeds a wood planer, was attempting to remove stacked wood fromthe planer feed with a crowbar. he lost his balance and fell toward the sharpener opening, which was 5 inches by 19 inches. while trying to stabilize his body, his hand went into the opening. the sharpener blade amputated employee #1's right hand above the wrist. there were no witnesses. employee #1 did not normally perform this task. he may have lost his balance because he was unfamiliar with the job. the piece of equipment was an s.a. wood planer from boston, massachusette s.

at approximately 2:30 p.m. on october 31, 1995, employee #1 and a coworker were loading a 43,000 lb steel coil onto the mandrel of slitting equipment. lateral straps holding the coil together were removed once it was loaded onto the transfer cart or coil buggy, which moves in two directions on tracks, toward and away from the mandrel. once the straps were removed, the steel in the middle of the coil sagged into the hollow opening. employee #1 grabbed a short stick that was in the area and pushed the sagging material, or steel tail, back up and out of the way so that the coil could slide onto the stationary mandrel shaft. as the tailwas being held, another coworker operated the coil buggy from controls located on the opposite side where he could not observe the coworker with the stick. as the buggy was moving in toward the mandrel, employee #1 was crushed between the coil and the adjacent slitter machinery. he suffered severe internal injuries an d died approximately one week later.

on october 1 1985 at 8:05 an employee #1 was climbing a diagonal 45% beam from elev 1071 to roof elev 1093. he became dizzy and fell approx 8' to 10' to the horizontal members. employee suffered laceration bleeding, multiple stitches over left eye. torn groin artery on left side, tear in liver causing internal bleeding and several areas of bruises on his left side. employee unwent surgery to fix tear in liver and will be hospitalized for an extended period.

employee #1 had retired from the company, but was rehired as a consultant to help remove a pug mill from an abndoned plant. a 10 foot by 14 foot metal roll-up door had to be removed in order to get the pug mill out. after the pug mill was removed the metal door was picked up by a boom-equipped lift truck. the door had been secured on one side only. employee #1 was apparently outside the building. he entered the building by walking under the door at about the same time that the door came loose at the top. the door fell, striking employee #1 around the shoulders and upper part of the body, killing him.

an employee of an insulator manufacturer touched a metal chain hanging from an insulator. the chain was used for test purposes after the insulator was deenergized. (the test passed 100 milliamperes through the circuit.) the chain had a residual charge on it when the employee contacted it, and he received an electric shock.

the pressure relief valve between the iso stripper and the blowdown line failed on sunday, december 16. the failed valve was removed on the following tuesday. the blowdown line on the iso stripper side was to be closed off with a block valve until the relief valve could be replaced. there was a power outage at the platformer unit which caused the sulfer unit to shut down. a block valve at the platformer was opened, causing hydrogen sulfide to vent into the blowdown line. hydrogen sulfide vented from the open end of the blowdown line at the iso stripper because the block valve had not been correctly installed (no blind was installed) . the last time the two employees were seen before the accident they were wearing their air masks. when the employees were found after the accident, employee #2 had his mask partially on and was located approximately 8 feet north of the work area. employee #1 was on the receiver, approximately 15 feet northwest of the work area, and was not wearing an air mask. the temperature on day of the accide nt was 13 degrees f. there was no spotter assigned to assist the employees in the event of an emergency.
employee #1 was laying brick from the top of a 5 buck scaffold when a windstorm blew the scaffold over. the employee died.

an employee was operating a rock drill. he was moving the drill while the boom was extended upward about 45 feet above the ground. the boom contacted a 19.5-kilovolt-to-ground overhead power line located about 35 feet above the ground. the employee was electrocuted when he got out of the rock drill's cab.

at approximately 12:00 noon on july 22, 1995, excess carbon had built up on the burner of a water heater, causing it to malfunction and produce carbon monoxide gas. the gas contaminated and saturated the ventilation systems room and was drawn into the makeup air of the hvac system. the hvac system distributed the contaminated air throughout the building. employees #1 and #2 experienced symptoms such as nausea, headaches, and blurred vision in their effort to evacuate inmates who were experiencing similar symptoms.

on december 12, 1996, employee #1, a carpenter, was on a forklift work platform installing plywood fascia at the eave line. he lost power to his circular saw an d he was unable to get the forklift operator's attention. employee #1 then decided to access the 4:12 pitched roof and walk along the peak to an opening 80 feetaway, where he could enter the building and check the power. he slipped on the frosty plywood sheeting near the peak, slid approximately 25 feet, and fell thre e stories to muddy ground. he was hospitalized with a fractured pelvis and tailbone.

employee #1 was welding on a bottom plate of a company's #84 packer head machine . the hydraulics were not locked out and the approximately 5,000 lb packer headfell on the employee, crushing and killing him. the employee was performing his normal job duties at the time of the accident.

y 100 feet into a river. his body was not recovered. the top of the tank has not been found. the tank had not been checked for possible explosive fumes. neither employee was wearing a safety belt or tie off. welding of the catwalk could have been done at any time during the previous week before startup. a blueprint draftsman stated that the welding was not something that was needed to start up production. according to a sales rep and parts order person, there had never been a policy of testing tanks before welding.

employee #1 was part of a five-man crew that was laying metal decking on bar joists; he and his foreman were sorting a bundle of decking. some of the sheets were to be used to finish the area on which they were currently working and others were to be used in the next area. decking could not be laid under the bundle, so an opening had been left in the roof deck . employee #1 was holding one end of a sheet of decking, and the foreman was holding the other end. the foreman feltthe sheet of decking move and turned to look. he saw employee #1 fall 20.2 feetthrough the opening in the metal decking to the ground. he was killed.

a delivery person for a brick and stone supply company was using a boom on the back of his flatbed truck to unload brick at a construction site. a 7200-volt overhead power line was located nearby, and the sun blinded the employee's view of the power line. a contractor at the site warned the employee about the power line; but minutes later the employee, who was standing on the ground operating the boom controls, brought the boom into contact with the line. the employee was electrocuted.

employee #1, an ornamental iron worker, was installing clips on a structural steel framework preparatory to the attachment of a decorative metal framework for glass. he was killed when he fell approximately 12 feet to an adjacent floor leve l from the h-beam on which he was standing. the employee was wearing a safety be lt but was not tied off. employee #1 had undergone safety training. the safety policy regarding tying off was enforced and a safety belt was provided. the accident was due to an isolated incident of employee misconduct.

employee #1 was a member of a water tank (tanker) crew and a fire fighter for the bureau of land management. while he was on duty, the fire tank of his truck developed a leak in the rear bulkhead area. he consulted with his supervisor, who decided that the employee would repair the leaks. this required him to enter the water truck's tank, grind off paint, and weld (mig) the cracks. during the firs t phase of the job, employee #1 passed out for a few seconds, revived, went to lunch, and returned to the tank. after about eight minutes of grinding and welding, he passed out again and never regained consciousness. he was pronounced deadapproximately two hours later at the hospital.

employee #1 was struck by a backhoe/loader as it was backing up. he fell beneath the tires and was killed. the backhoe had an operating back-up alarm and had traveled approximately 100 ft before the accident occurred.

employee #1, an iron worker for approximately 15 to 20 years, and coworkers were bolting steel on top of a precipitator building under construction. at break time, employee #1 removed his safety belt and lanyard and laid down on an 18-in.-wide steel beam. he either rolled over or slipped while trying to get up and fell 68 ft to a metal grate floor, sustaining a fractured and dislocated knee. he was hospitalized for his injuries.

employee #1 was threading a cross perf unit of a printing press. the webbing had developed a tear so he completely tore it across in order to reweb it. he inser ted the paper between the anvil and the perf blade, holding the cylinder with his left hand, and began to jog a switch that activated the cylinders. the nip point was not guarded and his finger became caught between the rotating cylinders. his left index finger was amputated when he tried to pull it out of the equipmen

on december 19, 1997, employee #1 was using a webb form fabrication machine. he was inserting a flat steel sheet when his glove became caught in the roller. his hand was pulled in before he could release the foot switch and trip the wire cut-off. employee #1's left little finger, ring finger, and the first digit of his index finger were amputated.

fourteen federal government employees (five from the bureau of land management a nd nine from the forest service) were fighting a forest fire near glenwood springs, colorado, when they were overcome by rapidly advancing smoke and flames. all 14 employees died.

employee #1 was standing on an 8 ft ladder installing sheetrock nailers and hurricane clips on a ceiling adjacent to an unguarded porch. employee #1 either stepped or fell off the ladder and fell off the porch to the ground. employee #1 hithis head on a concrete wall and died.

on july 22, 1994, while operating a forklift in a warehouse, employee #1 struck vertically stored material. he got off the machine to straighten the stowage. when the stored material fell onto him, he was unable to move the load. employee # 1 suffered blunt trauma to the chest and died.

at approximately 8:10 a.m. on march 28, 1994, employee #1 was on top of a 124 in . high mixer cleaning it with a brush. after he had been brushing a while, employee #1 called down to a coworker and asked him to activate the machine. the coworker could not see employee #1. when the mixer was activated, another coworker yelled out that employee #1 was in the machine. the first coworker immediately de -activated the machine. employee #1 had been caught and crushed between the mixer blades and the mixer walls. he was pronounced dead at the scene.

at approximately 3:20 p.m. on april 27, 1994, employees #2, #3, #4, #8, and #9, iron workers, were working 35 ft above the ground on top of a single-story steelwarehouse that was under construction. employees #1, #5, #6, and #7 were working on the structure's ground level. dark clouds appeared in the distance to the west of the construction site. employees #2, #3, #4, #8, and #9 were instructed to come down from the structure because a storm was approaching. suddenly, a strong gust of wind hit the structure and it collapsed. employee #1 was struck by a vertical steel column, which crushed his chest, killing him. employees #2 through #9 received various minor and serious injuries. employee #7 was hospitalized overnight.

on november 2, 2001, employee #1 was working alone on private property operating a smooth wheel roller to smooth out gravel. he was found by one of his employees that had been working at another site. it appears that employee #1was thrown off of the roller after the wheels got too far off of the road. the employee was apparently crushed by the roller as it overturned. no witnesses observed the incident.

a contracting crew was constructing a pole barn. the foreman was working on the crest of the barn, and one of the other employees was working along the edge, nailing a brace. the last employee was on the ground, handing up materials. the employee who was nailing the brace contacted an overhead power line with his hip. he then grabbed the conductor with his right hand so that he could move away. he lost his balance and then hooked his left arm over another conductor. he was electrocuted and fell 13 feet to the ground.

employee #1 was removing wrappers from a gram 10-wide wrapping machine for ice cream bars. the guard was not placed in front of the shears and the tip of her right index finger was amputated. there was no interlock system to shut off the wrapping machine if a guard had been removed.

several employees were reskinning a metal roof which measured 84 feet by 48 feet , and was located 20 feet above a concrete floor. the roof contained 8 uncovered fiberglass skylights, 11 feet by 3 feet. employee #1, the foreman, had warned the other employees to be careful around the skylights. the job was 50% complete. it was a friday, and the employees had started to clean up. employee #1 dumped the water cooler and started to walk across the roof. he stepped onto a skylightand fell through it to the concrete floor. he died of massive head injuries. the skylights were not covered or otherwise guarded, and were painted the same color as the roof. also, it was found that employee #1's blood alcohol was .153.

an employee of safeway stores incorporated was climbing down the built-in ladder on top of a milk truck trailer. he slipped on the third or fourth rung, (rungs are 12 in. apart), and fell against a platform railing and onto a platform located next to the truck. platform was 63 in. high, while the top of the milk traile r was about 44 in. above the platform. as a result, the employee injured his back and was hospitalized for treatment of a strain or sprain.

at approximately 12:30 p.m. on march 19, 1987, employee #1 was falling and bucking timber on a hillside. employee #1 had fell a large fir tree, which rolled down the hill and struck a maple tree approximately 90 feet in length; fracturing a pproximately the top 55 feet of the maple. employee #1 bucked off a 26-foot-leng th log of the fir next to the maple. at this time the fractured top of the maple tree fell and struck employee #1 on the head and right shoulder, killing him in stantly. the county sheriff was dispatched and employee #1 was pronounced dead a t the scene. the bucking of the fir tree while it was in contact with the fractured maple may have jarred it and caused the fractured top to fall.
employee #1 was pressurizing a tire to seat the bead on the rim when the rim exploded. employee #1 was killed when he was struck in the forehead by flying piece s of the exploding rim.

an employee was filling a dayton gasoline-powered generator with 1 gallon of gasoline from a 2.5-gallon non-approved spoutless gas can. he spilled gasoline over the generator's fuel tank and onto the motor and wiring. he replaced the fuel cap and started the generator. sparks from the wiring or spark plug ignited the gasoline vaors and set fire to the generator. the portable generator measured about 30 inches long by 14 inches high by 17 inches wide, and it had a 1 gallon gastank. (it was a dayton model 159, 3500 watt, 240 volt; sn 3w0141.) the weather was 88 degrees f, high humidity, winds 4 - 8 mph (from airport weather report). the injured employee sustained first degree burns on his right hand, wrist, and forearm. there was no lost time. the employee was a glazier. refueling the gener ator was part of his job. he had earlier asked about the whereabouts of the spout but had used the can without the spout anyhow.
employee #1 was mowing a steep embankment, using a tractor not equipped with a rops and seat belt. the tractor overturned onto the employee, killing him.

employees #1 and #2 were working in the bottom of a trench 12 feet deep and approximately 15 feet wide by 35 feet long. employees #1 and #2 were using a jack hammer to cut open a storm sewer so that a parking lot drain could be hooked intothe storm sewer. the unshored walls of the trench, which consisted of well round ed gravel and sand, caved in on the employees. employee #1 was completely buried and died of asphyxia. employee #2 was buried up to his chest but was rescued, hospitalized, and treated for a sprained ankle, cuts, body bruising.

an employee was hauling dirt from one location to another at a construction site . he backed in at the dumping location and unloaded the raised dump truck bed. a fter he dumped the load, he pulled forward, and the truck bed contacted an overhead power line. the employee jumped out of the truck and was electrocuted.

three carpenters were placing 4-ft by 8-ft plywood decking on a townhouse roof that was 30 ft, 2 in. from the ground. employee #1 was supplying plywood panels to the other two carpenters who were working on the north side of the roof peak. employee #1 dropped a panel that was to be placed at the roof peak. the panel fell back toward him on his side of the roof slope, knocking him off his feet and causing him to land on top of the panel. the employee slid down the south roof slope, riding on the plywood panel with his hands clutching the upper edge of the panel. the bottom of the plywood panel struck a 2-ft by 4-in. toe board that was installed approximately 18 in. from the roof edge. when the plywood hit the to e board, it forced the plywood with employee #1 hanging on it, to fall backward over the roof edge to the concrete, resulting in the death of employee #1.

all the employees were performing their normal jobs (i.e. punch press operators, lift truck operators, parts cleaners). five propane lift trucks, which were badly tuned, were being operated in a closed warehouse with no ventilation. carboxyhemoglobin levels of the employees sent to the hospital ranged from 13 to 19%. the blood samples tested were drawn after an eight hour shift. all employees sent to the emergency room were released within three hours.

the shop foreman was inventorying the stock in the scrap paper storage area wher e the forklift operator was using a caterpillar, model v50, forklift to load brown scrap paper bundles onto a truck going to a recycle yard. the brown scrap paper storage row had a forward lean of approximately 10 degrees on stacks of six bundles. the forklift operator had loaded about half the truck and was loading a three-bundle stack onto the truck when employee #1 came into the area to talk with the shop foreman. the stack of six bundles behind the one being loaded fell forward onto employee #1. employee #1 was struck and fatally crushed by one or two bundles of brown cardboard scrap paper, each weighing approximately 1,140 lb.

on august 20, 2001, employee #1, a greenhouse employee, was positioning potted plants on a nursery growth table near a large 48-in. ventilation fan. as employee #1 stood on the table, she contacted the rotating blades that pulled her into the fan. the rotating blades lacerated her abdomen and lower chest, causing massi

at approximately 9:30 to 9:45 p.m. on may 11, 1988, second shift employees #1 through #8 were evacuated from a warehouse/manufacturing plant after experiencing headaches, nausea, dizziness, and weakness. employees #1, #2, and #3 were takenby the rescue squad to the pitt memorial hospital emergency room for observation and treatment. employees #4 through #8 were brought to the ayden fire department for observation by the county medical officer. during the first and second shifts, three propane-powered forklifts had been used frequently inside the closed building, which has a 100 percent recirculated air conditioning unit.

employee #1 was using a drott 2500 cruz-crane, serial #154, to position a 40,000 -lb concrete mixer onto some heavy timber dunnage. coworkers were on the ground guiding the mixer into place. the crane was level, with its outriggers fully extended on pads of heavy 12-in. by 12in. timbers and all four wheels completely of f the ground. according to the boom angle indicator, the boom was extended 50 ftat a 71 degree angle. the mixer was supposedly out over the right front outrigger (approximate radius of 19 ft 6 in.), although eyewitnesses stated that the mixer was actually touching the outrigger. the mixer was approximately 6 in. off the ground when a loud pop was heard. employee #1 looked around trying to identify what he had heard, and then began to stand up. suddenly the crane cab broke loose from the carriage, turning on its right side with such force that the operator was ejected. he landed on his upper body, approximately 20 ft from the crane' s carriage. employee #1 sustained multiple fractures, including cervical disc fractures and massive chest contusions. he was killed. a check of the crane load capacity chart indicated that there was an overload factor of approximately 6,000 lb. a metallurgical analysis of the turntable area indicated stress faults in the turntable ring and in some of the bolts fastening the cab to the carriage. apparently the lift was opposite the faulty area of the turntable, which, combined with the overload factor, resulted in this accident.

at approximately 4:00 p.m. on july 9, 1985, employee #1 and a coworker were cleaning windows at the first federal savings and loan building in roanoke, va. whenemployee #1 started to lower himself over the side of the building to clean the seventh story windows, he began to fall and yelled for help. the coworker lower ed himself to the ground so that he could get back to the roof to help employee #1, but by the time he arrived on the roof employee #1 had reached the street. he was treated by rescue personnel and transported to roanoke memorial hospital, where he was admitted for treatment of multiple fractures. the friction box was threaded backward, and when employee #1's weight was applied the rope came loose , allowing him to fall.

employees #1 and #2 were working 20 to 30 feet below a 10 foot high by 21 foot long cinderblock parapet wall. high winds ranging between 40 and 50 mph winds caused the wall to fail. employees #1 and #2 were struck by material from the falling wall; both were hospitalized.

at 5:50 p.m. on august 25, 1995, the #1 paper machine broke its paper while wind ing from one roll to another at the boise cascade paper mill in st. helens, or. employee #1 went to the h1 winder to feed the paper into the rolls so he could splice it back together. in the process of feeding the paper, he leaned over the approximately 2 ft roll of paper but could not reach the paper end, so he stepped up onto a 17 in. roller to gain height. the machine was being operated by a coworker who had it in the winding mode, which means the rolls were turning at 12. 6 rpm. while employee #1 was reaching to feed the paper, his foot became caughtin the pinch point of the two rolls, breaking a bone in his foot and his ankle before the coworker could shut off the machine. the coworker and another coworker lifted and assisted employee #1 out of the machine. the coworker then called for 911 and his supervisor. at approximately 6:25 p.m., the medics took employee # 1 to immanuel hospital. as of 1:00 p.m. on august 29, 1995, employee #1 had had two operations and was expected to leave the hospital on august 31.

employee #1, a highway maintenance worker, was part of an 11-man crew performing road maintenance work on sr 249. employee #1 was acting as flagman and had beeninstructed to work near the grader machine, warning the operator of approaching vehicles and controlling the traffic flow past the equipment that was working on the shoulder. employee #1 had been near the grader machine at the northern endof the dot work site. a sha dump truck, #036, had driven to the northern end an d the grader had moved along the north shoulder toward the southern end of the work area. employee #1 apparently had started walking south along the shoulder to ward the grader when truck #036 started to back up. the driver, who did not seeemployee #1, traveled 50 ft backward, running over employee #1. he sustained massive crushing injuries to the head and chest, and was killed. employee #1 was trained in his work assignment and had been an sha employee for 14 1/2 years. the employer provides all personal protective equipment required for its work assignments. at the time of the accident the weather was overcast with light rain falling.

employee #1 and coworkers were replastering a swimming pool. employee #1 was mixing plaster when he spilled calcium chloride on his tennis shoes. his tennis shoes became wet from a subsequent rainfall and after they dried out employee #1'sfoot became painful. he removed his shoes and realized that all his toes and part of his left foot were red and inflamed. employee #1 flushed his feet with wate r and went to his doctor later that day.

employee #1 stepped out into the middle of a road that was approximately 50 ft from a driveway that he was about to start blacktopping. he did not hear or pay a ttention to the back-up alarm of a dump truck loaded with blacktop that was back ing down the same road. the truck driver did not see employee #1 and ran over him, killing him.
employee #1 was on a scaffold positioned atop an air shaft, removing asbestos from overhead. he fell 56 ft from the scaffold and was killed.

employee #1 was pulling rough-cut pieces of pine 4 by 4's from a chain conveyoronto a hydraulic lift when he became ill. the temperature at the time the employee developed symptoms was within recommended limits for healthy, acclimatized workers. no citation was issued.

several employees were rigging a 3,175 pound piece of concrete so that it couldbe hoisted to the second floor. the piece of concrete had been part of a stock chest. the foreman checked their rigging job and told them to re-rig it. he thenleft the area. the employees then used a 12 foot choker sling attached to the chain hoist hook. when they started to lift the concrete, the sling slipped and provided enough slack so that the concrete slab could swing around and fall onto employee #1. he was pinned between the concrete and a small wall. the other employee (employee #2) received minor injuries when he scrambled to get out of the way of the falling concrete section.
on january 18, 1994, while removing roof sheets, employee #1 fell 14 feet through the roof to the concrete ground. employee #1 died on january 31 from his headinjuries.

working for an electric utility, a three-person tree-trimming crew was trimming trees near overhead power lines. the crew foreman was about 30 feet above the ground, secured in a large tree by a rope and safety belt. the rope was securely tied to the tree. a limb came off the trunk of the tree and fell over the top of one of the 7620-volt power lines. (the limb over the power line was about 7 to 8 feet above the line and beyond it by about 3 to 4 feet, with branches and leave s still attached.) the foreman began cutting away branches, first by handsaw, then by chainsaw, then by handsaw again. he was using the handsaw to trim the limbover the power line. the foreman tied the pull rope to a limb. the employees on the ground, upon instruction of the foreman, pulled the rope, which passed through the top of the tree. at the same time, the foreman started to cut underneath the limb. the limb was pulled into the power line. the foreman received an electric shock, perhaps by direct contact with the line, which caused him to lose his balance and fall. his fall was broken by the safety belt and line. however, he had been electrocuted. burn marks were found on his right shoulder and leg.

at approximately 3:30 p.m. on june 8, 1994, employee #1 was loading rolls of paper onto the back of a flatbed truck using a hyster lift truck that had a clamp a ttachment. as he attempted to make a sharp turn with the clamp raised about 5 ft , the lift truck tipped over, and employee #1 was thrown out of the truck's cab. he was caught under the clamp attachment and killed. the lift truck was equipped with seat belts, but employee #1was not wearing one. company policy does not require that employees wear seat belts.

employee #1 was removing axles and wheels from the underside of a used house trailer. the trailer was being set up on the company sales lot. the trailer had been placed on concrete blocks, but not in conformance with applicable florida state law. (the blocks were not stacked with the open cells in the vertical position .) as a result, the blocks at the left rear of the trailer collapsed, dropping the trailer onto employee #1. he was in a kneeling position under the left side of the trailer. he suffered multiple fractures and was asphyxiated.

employee #1, a warehouse operator, dropped his safety glasses into the compartment of a railroad tank car that was being filled with aerosol. the aerosol was blown through a line by low pressure nitrogen gas. after employee #1 notified coworkers and his supervisor, the material flow was turned off but not the gas. wearing a gas mask, employee #1 entered the railcar through a manhole. he was overcome and fell to the bottom of the tank. employee #2, his supervisor, entered the railcar in a rescue attempt but was also overcome. two other employees wearing gas masks attempted an unsuccessful rescue. coworkers who donned scba removed the two bodies. neither employee #1 nor employee #2 could be revived. two hours later the tank air was tested and found to be at 14 percent oxygen.

employee #1 was unloading stock from a truck trailer. there were approximately twenty gas cylinders, labeled poisonous, lying on their sides. employee #1 heard a hissing noise and contacted his supervisor, employee #2. employee #2 contacted his supervisor, employee #4. employee #4 contacted his supervisor, employee #3. the cylinder was leaking nitric oxide. the trailer was immediately closed and removed by the employees. all four exposed employees were not experiencing symptoms, but were sent as a precaution to the emergency room. they were admitted over night for observation.
at approximately 9:00 a.m. on september 28, 1988, employee #1, a roofer, was walking along the edge of a roof carrying clay roofing tile. he lost his balance an d fell. employee #1 was hospitalized.

employee #1 was using a powered horizontal conveyor to weigh and band 12,000 pound brass coils. the coils are moved by paddles on an i-beam under the conveyor rollers. the machine was either running or accidentally started while employee #1 was reaching between two coils. his head and right shoulder were crushed and employee #1 was found dead several minutes later.

employee #1 had delivered a piece of construction equipment known as a road wide ner to a job site and then parked it. when he dismounted, the road widener beganto roll, crushing him against a chain-link fence. employee #1 was killed.

at 4pm on january 16, 1986, two employees were lowering equipment from the roofof a 50 foot high building to the ground when the hoist they were using was pulled off the roof. the two employees fell with the hoist. employee #1 was killed a nd employee #2 suffered multiple fractures.
a 33-year-old electrician was found unconscious on a catwalk in the area where he had been changing fuses. he died of a heart attack, which may have been caused by electric shock.

employee # 1 and coworkers were loading rental components, including tools and a tool box, onto a float during the rigging down operation of an oil well drilling unit. when employee #1 released the dogs on the load line of the a-frame-typeboom, the boom line winch began to freewheel and the boom fell. it struck employee #1, killing him. the boom was part of an oil field service semi-truck.

on or about february 7, 1995, employee #1, while gathering material for a job, fell from a 16 ft metal extension ladder inside a warehouse. there were no eyewitnesses to the accident. on or about march 1, 1995, the employee died from injuries sustained in the accident..

employee #1, an iron worker, had just returned from lunch and was walking along a steel girder to start decking on the south side of the girder. as he was walking, he grabbed his side but did not try to catch himself before falling 24 ft 2 1/2 in. onto a concrete floor. his head struck a concrete slab, and he sustained massive head injuries and a broken neck. employee #1 was killed.

an employee on a surveying crew was holding a prism range pole. it was a clear day, but there was water on the ground. the pole was about 6 meters long and was constructed of conductive materials. the employee was standing about 1.5 meters to the north of a 13-kilovolt overhead power line. the pole contacted the power line, and the employee was electrocuted.

employees #1 and #2 were in front of a manway opening to a process vessel at a refinery. the vessel contained pyrophoric materials and flammable gas. employee # 1 was standing on a platform directly in front of the manway, washing out the vessel with a water hose, when there was an explosion inside the tank. the force of the explosion blew employee #1 65 ft away, killing him. employee #2 was injure d, but not hospitalized.
an electrician working from an aerial lift was installing an electrical box and conduit on a utility pole. he contacted a 480-volt overhead power line and was electrocuted.

employee #1 was moving a work platform. he had gotten the angle clips and was getting into the basket of a nisso america, inc., aerial lift (model "angel's wing s") when the tube steel broke the welds and came loose, falling to the steel below. employee #1 fell out of the basket and struck the tube steel as he fell to the concrete basement floor. employee #1 was killed.

an employee, the manager of a small retail battery-charging store, had rewired four wall-mounted battery chargers. he had left off the vinyl insulators to the a lligator clips that attach to the batteries. perhaps thinking the power was off, he took hold of two of the uninsulated clips and was electrocuted. the diode rectifier in the charger was passing 120 volts, ac, into the dc ouput. witnesses stated that the employee had burns on both hands. the employer stated that the deceased had had years of experience working with this type equipment.

two employees were installing three 50-ampere, 480-volt, three-phase, four-conductor receptacles for some welding machines. the employees mislabeled the cables and placed the cable marked as #2 in switch #3 and the cable marked as #3 in switch #2. one of the employees was still working on one of the misnumbered cables when the switch was closed. the employee was electrocuted.

employee #1 had just finished placing a case 580d backhoe on a trailer and was preparing to secure it when he noticed the boom was not straight. he then walked to the rear of the backhoe, placing himself between the boom and outrigger. he reached through the window to pull the control lever to straighten the boom. he may have pulled the wrong lever, because the boom forced him against the outrigger, inflicting severe head and chest trauma. he died.

two power line workers were connecting an underground electrical service lateral to a newly constructed house. one of them was working at a pad-mounted transformer; the other was installing a meter at the house, about 70 meters away. the employee at the 7200-volt, 50-kilovolt-ampere general electric transformer (modelno. 12740, serial no. p370332-yzb) was wearing class 0, type 1, north brand rubber insulating gloves. he was working on the 120/240-volt connections at the transformer. when the employee at the house returned to the transformer, he found his coworker kneeling in front of the transformer. he noticed that his coworker'sface was blue and that he had stopped breathing. the injured employee had appare ntly contacted exposed live parts and had been electrocuted.

an employee was tightening bolts on a steel column located next to the runway conductors for an overhead crane. the employee laid on the column to reach a boltand contacted the 240-volt runway conductors. he was electrocuted.

employees #1 and #2 were working under a suspended mobile home. the hoist chaingave way and dropped the ceiling section of the mobile home onto them, crushing them. employees #1 and #2 were hospitalized.

injured employee was using a 12" table saw, equipped with a rip blade. he was at tempting to trim about 1/4" off of a strip, 2 1/2" wide x 3/4" thick. the saw guard was not being used. the employee set the guide (fence) and proceeded to push the strip, which was approximately 24" long, along the edge of the blade using his right hand. employee's hand and right thumb came into contact with the saw blade, causing partial amputation of side of thumb.

at 2:33 p.m. on november 29, 1984, employee #1, age 17, was loading wooden table s onto a slat bed conveyor. he had on tennis shoes with long shoelaces. when he placed his left foot on the lower, returning surface of the conveyor, his shoelace became caught in the nearby sprocket and his left foot was pulled into the catch point, fracturing his ankle. the catch point was guarded immediately after the accident. see osha form no. 89a, this report number, for photos.

employee #1 had climbed a ladder up to a platform (12' x 66' x 8") above the concrete floor. some wooden prehung doors were to be handed up to him. on the far side of the 12' wide platform was a floor opening for spiral stairs, 4' wide x 78 " long. the opening had been left uncovered from the previous days work. employee #1 had just received and set the prehung door down on the platform, when he apparently turned and stepped into the floor opening. he landed on his hands and face, striking his forehead. co-workers came to his aid and called an ambulance. it was apparent his wrists were broken , and blood was coming from his nostrils. he died two days later as a result of complications from a skull fracture, according to the olmstead county coroner.

employee #1 was boning hams on a conveyor line in a meat packing plant. he used his knife to pull a ham from the line toward him. the knife slipped out of the ham, causing the employee to inadvertently pull the knife toward himself and cuthimself in the left eye. the employer has a written rule against using knives to pull meat off the line. the employees are provided wiyh hooks to pull meat. the y are also allowed to pull meat with their hands.

at 4:40 pm on november 2, 1984, employee #1 and three other employees were working cutting logs. the first assistant was working at the log deck positioning logs for dogging onto the carriage. the second man was acting as an off-bearer, removing slabs as they were cut from the logs. the third assistant was receiving the slabs from the off-bearer for stacking or scrap piling. employee #1 was in position and was operating the carriage control lever. he had just powered the carriage, with a 12 foot long log, through the circular 54 inch diameter saw blade ( 600 rpm unloaded). the sawed off slab fell away from the saw blade onto it's rounded side. the off bearer was maneuvering the slab that it could be received by the stacking employee. while this was happening employee #1 had returned the carriage to the log-load position and was reaching to his right, probably to releas e the dog so the log could be turned on the carriage or to grasp the ratchet handle to move the log further into the saw path for an additional cut. by moving the carriage to the log-load position employee #1 had exposed the entire top halfof the rotating sawblade. this procedure constituted a violation of his standard procedures. the log or cant is supposed to be left in position so that the sawblade cannot be contacted by a slab at least to a height equal to the diameter of the log or cant. by conducting interviews, simulating events and assuming certain reasonable actions we can arrive at a probable course of events. as employee #1 was reaching to the right his body was in line with the saw blade and he was looking away from the saw blade and the off-bearer. the log deck assistant was getting another log from the log deck for mounting on the carriage. the off-bearer was maneuvering the slab of wood. as he was maneuvering the slab, one end of the slab contacted the saw blade. the saw blade yanked the slab from the off-bearer's hands and forced it between the bottom rotating blade and the 36" top sawblade. the 36 inch blade was not rotating. the off-bearer heard the noise of the slab being caught and looked around in time to see employee #1 being struck by the flying slab.

employee #1 was assigned to replace a timing chain on an injection molding machine. he removed the guard covering the timing chain and replaced the timing chain . they turned the power to the machine back on and adjusted the timing chain. he went to another injection molding machine and got it running. as he was leaving the room he remembered that he had not replaced the guard for the timing machine. employee #1 replaced the guard and tried to install a bolt on the top of the guard. when the machine cycled, his hand was caught by the chain. he suffered a fractured hand and cuts on the hand.

employee #1 died of smoke inhalation. he was reported to have been using an acetylene torch while doing automobile repair work. it is believed that he accidently upset a nearby can of enamel thinner which ignited from the torch flame. it is also believed that there was a natural gas leak inside of the building which exploded when the fire erupted. employee #2 was in the office at the front of the building when the fire erupted. he attempted to go into the shop. when he saw the intensity of the fire, he decided to leave the building. his hair was singed a nd his hand cut while he was fleeing the building. he did not see a doctor.

an employee using rubber gloves was working on a 13.8-kilovolt (phase to phase) circuit from a fiberglass insulated bucket of a hydraulic aerial lift (1974 telbow: model t45 169 ae) mounted on a 1974 international truck. the employee's upper arm made accidental contact with the top side of a lightning arrester (entry point at 7980 volts phase to ground) while his right abdomenal area was in contact with a bare conductor running to the grounded neutral (exit point). the employee was wearing gloves, but not sleeves. he was electrocuted.

an employee had just completed splicing a telecommunications cable. this cable was located on a pole also used to support three 7200-volt feeder lines. upon completing the splice, the employee went down a ladder to retrieve a protective device (boot) to cover the splice. once the employee got this boot, he climbed back up the ladder. during the crossover from the ladder to the top of the conduit stacks, or possibly while making himself more comfortable on these stacks, he accidentally came in contact with the bottom threads of the live center cutout. the point of contact was his left hand. the current exited through the top of his left thigh and through the the left side of his head. the employee then fell 23 feet to the ground. the injured employee was in ventricular fibrillation but was revived by a worker nearby. a hard hat, safty belt, and lanyard were not used; a nd the employee had not received any special training in working near overhead power lines.

employee fell 9ft (approx) from the second buck level of a tubular wel- ded scaffold he was engaged in erecting. he was walking on (2) two 2x10 inch planks. the planks had been previously installed on (3) three outrigger brackets--(1) one of the planks was displaced causing the employee to fall to a concrete floor. the scaffold planks were not secured and the fall resulted in multiple fractures to both of the employees arms.

an employee had been using a welder. he was perspiring and was well grounded through his abdomen. as he was replacing the welder's electrode, the employee apparently received a fatal electric shock. it is not known whether he was wearing gloves at the time of the accident.

the employees of two subcontractors were 19 ft above floor level, soldering a condensate line to the vent of two 36,000 lb combustible liquid storage tanks thatwere in use. one employee was using an acetylene turbo torch. ink varnish that had just been transferred into the tank was approximately 45 degrees fahrenheitabove its 215 fahrenheit flash point. twenty minutes later, during the soldering process, flammable vapors produced by the elevated temperature of the stored combustible liquid (which was mixed elsewhere in the plant) were ignited, causing a flashback in the tank and an explosion. employee #1, who had not been provided with fall protection, was thrown from the top of the tank to floor. employee #2 was on a mechanical lift that was next to the tank. several employees of the link company were affected by the blast and were hospitalized. no precautions had been taken or attempts made to eliminate ignition sources. the tanks were not emptied or inerted. employees of both subcontractors were unaware of the hazards of combustible liquids and vapors at elevated temperatures.
employee #1 was spray painting the interior of a 20,000 gallon water tank. an explosion occurred, killing the employee.

employee #1 was in his fourth month of a one year on-the-job training program to become a printer. he was monitoring the printed wallpaper and making adjustment s to ensure its quality. noticing that there was more color on the paper than there should have been, he picked up a small hand brush and started to clean the paint roller while the printer continued to operate. employee #1 became caught in the nip point between the print roller and the cylinder of the waldren surface printer, serial #1626. he broke two fingers and suffered extensive muscle damageand skin abrasions on both arms. employee #1 did not follow approved procedures or use approved equipment for this cleaning operation.

on may 2, 1985, an accident occurred on an oil rig which resulted in the death of one employee and serious injury to another. the employees had completed the hole and were coming out of the hole with the drill string when the fast line broke. the broken cable whipped around, striking employees #1 and 2.

an employee was on a utility pole to disconnect primary phase conductors. he use d a hot stick to remove the jumper conductors from all three phase conductors. one of the jumpers was stiff and remained near the primary phase conductor when it was disconnected. the employee then started to remove the jumper conductors from their respective cut-out switches. when he pulled on the stiff jumper, it dropped back into contact with the primary phase conductor. the employee was electrocuted.

employee #1, age 19, was working atop a sulfur conversion unit, washing out the interior with a hose that was stuck down an open manway. the unit was under a nitrogen purge and employee #1 was later discovered in the bottom of the unit. he died of asphyxia. employee #1 was working along and without respiratory protection was used. there were no eyewitnesses to the accident.
employee #1 drove his vacuum truck into a gas plant riser, causing an explosion and fire. there were no barricades or barriers to protect the riser. employee #1 was killed.
employee #1, age 19, was sent to bin #1 to see if the material was flowing. he either fell into the bin or the sand caved in on him. employee #1 died of suffocation.

three employees were raising a 40-foot ladder. one employee was holding the bottom of the ladder while the other two employees were pushing it up. as the ladder was being raised, it contacted a 7620-volt overhead power line. the two employees who were raising the ladder were electrocuted, and the employee who was holding the ladder received burns on both hands and both feet.

at 8:30 a.m. on april 17, 1984, employee #1 and coworkers were dismantling a pulp mill. the pulp mill platform was 50 ft square and 30 ft high, with 127 tons of concrete and iron. they were preparing to make a cut on the northwest column near the ground when the platform moved southward prematurely and collapsed. a piece of iron attached to the platform struck employee #1, smashing his head. he was killed.

employee #1 and a coworker were pipefitters assigned to repack the seal of a hydraulic cylinder in the coke guide on door machine #4. the foreman extended the coke guide so they could gain access to it. the coworker placed his padlock on the main electrical disconnect when it was in the nonenergized position. when he finished the work, he removed his padlock and put the disconnect back in the ener gized position. the coworker then returned to the coke guide area and asked employee #1 to make a last visual check of the job. when employee #1 completed the check and stood upright, the door machine and the coke guide with the extended guide moved, pinching him between the extended guide and a vertical support h-beam . the machine was moved by the door machine operator, who used the bypass jog control in conjunction with the throttle, bypassing the extended coke guide lockout control. the door machine operator misinterpreted the foreman's instructions, given at the lunch room, to use door machine #3.

an employee of an electric equipment manufacturing company was testing an energized transformer. the company's standard practice was to test the assembled units without electrical protective equipment, such as guards or covers. the employeecontacted an exposed live part and was electrocuted.

at about 1:30 pm on april 27, 1985, employee #1, a scuba diver, was working in 1 0 to 13 feet of water about 1500 feet offshore and east of st. johns island's no rth end, near vero beach, florida. a treasure hunting salvage boat, with 5 crewmembers, was digging holes by diverting propeller back-wash towards the ocean bottom, using a 90 degree angled tube. four anchors, two at the stern and two at the bow, kept the boat stationary while "blowing" holes. movement of the boat within a given salvage area was accomplished by winching specific anchor lines, so the anchors had to be relocated at frequent intervals. employee #1 was moving an anchor weighing about 15 pounds when, for unknown reasons, his air tank floated to the surface. a short time later, the employee surfaced in a state of panic. before 2 other crew members could reach him he sank to the bottom. the 2 crew me mbers brought him up from the bottom and put him on the boat. he was not breathing and had no pulse. they managed to revive him, and he was able to breathe on his own for about a half hour. when they were about 10 minutes from port he againstopped breathing, and was again revived. he was transported to the hospital by ambulance, where he died on may 3rd.

at approximately 3:10 p.m. on may 29, 1986, employee #1 was repairing leaks in the roof of the mississippi coliseum when it began to rain. as employee #1 and a coworker attempted to leave the roof, employee #1 began to slide down. he was unable to stop himself and slid off the roof, falling approximately 50 ft onto a canopy. he suffered serious injuries and died at 4:51 p.m.

the step. when the truck approached a collection point, employee #1 dismounted before the truck had stopped completely. employee #1 lost his footing on the roadway's loose rock surface and fell under the garbage truck's rear wheels. he was killed.

employee #1 was boning an end roast with a meat bandsaw. the employee did not adjust the guard that was in place and consequently severed his left middle finger . the employee was a 32 year old man with over 10 years of experience.

an employee was repairing and adjusting an air hose clamp connection located near the top right corner of a shoe molding machine manufactured by gesta spa italy . as he was reaching for the air connection, his abdomen made contact with a 600 -volt step-down transformer. (the transformer was located 36 inches above the floor, about 9 inches from the right side of the machine.) the employee was electrocuted.
employee #1, a 71-year-old self-employed mason, fell, striking his abdomen against an object. he died of asphyxia. no jurisdiction.
a concrete mixer truck fell into a trench while it was discharging concrete. the truck struck employee #1, who was working in the trench, and severed his left leg.

at approximately 11:00 a.m. on april 1, 1985, employee #1 was working in the bottom of a trench that was next to a roadway. the roadway side of the trench caved in, burying employee #1. he died of asphyxia. the trench sides were angled, but not shored. there were no witnesses to the accident.

a primary air fan, a motor, and a coal pulverizer at an electric power generation plant were running at maximum capacity. the system overloaded and shut down. the air pressure in the pulverizer overcame the decaying primary air fan pressure , causing the coal dust to back flow into the primary air fan shroud, which had been running at 260 degrees celsius. upon contacting the hot metal, the coal dust gasified and ignited. the inboard side of the fan shroud separated and the blast from the explosion burned three employees who were working nearby. two of the m were hospitalized for their injuries.

employee #1 was driving a 1969 gmc side-loading garbage truck 7500 series, license #lg 7589, for a local municipality. this truck had several mechanical defects , and on the day of the accident was overheating. it had also developed carburetor trouble: the spring that controls the idling speed had apparently broken and the engine was accelerating at speeds that were too high. employee #1 and a coworker stopped at the local fire station to call a mechanic to check the vehicle. the vehicle was being worked on inside the fire station when the accident occurred. the automatic transmission on the truck was also defective; the starter would activate the truck even when the transmission was engaged in a forward or reve rse gear. the coworker was asked to start the vehicle, and as soon as he started it and stepped off, the vehicle started to roll forward. employee #1 was reaching over the right front wheel for the ignition switch, the brake switch, or the transmission shift levers, when the rotating wheel pulled him under. he was run over by the front and rear wheels and sustained severe crushing injuries to his neck and chest. employee #1 was killed.

employee #1 was operating a rousselle full revolution power press, company #16, which was provided with hand restraints. company policy is that the restraints a re checked and adjusted daily by the supervisor and initialed by the operator. however, this was not done on the day of the accident. employee #1 did not have the restraints adjusted properly. they allowed her to reach into the point of operation. she received a laceration to her right thumb, requiring 3 stitches to close, when the die descended. the company could have prevented this accident by following its own procedures for daily adjustment of the hand restraints.

an employee raised a 30-foot length of 5-inch-diameter aluminum irrigation pipeto dislodge a rabbit. the pipe contacted an overhead power line that was 21 feet , 7 inches above the ground. the employee was electrocuted.

at 1:58 a.m. on february 18, 1991, employee #1 suffered a massive heart attack. after a prolonged period of treatment and hospitalization, the employee died on april 2, 1991, at riverside hospice. no investigation was conducted.

at approximately 10:53 a.m. on july 4, 1998, employee #1 was attempting to openthe northwest remote-controlled gate to enter a facility. when the gate opened, employee #1 became caught between the stationary upright of the chain-link fence and the gate. he was crushed and killed.

employee #1 was helping her husband back a truck into loading dock #2 by communicating with him over a hand-held walkie-talkie. she was moving south, toward the north side of a williams trucking tractor trailer that was parked in loading do ck #3. the williams truck began to pull away from the loading dock, turning sharply south-southwest. according to witnesses, employee #1 walked into an approximately 3 ft gap between the rear of the truck cab sleepover compartment and the right rear truck tires. she was run over and killed.

at approximately 12:00 noon on april 27, 1998, employee #1, a seam welder/decoiler operator, was placing a roll of coiled steel onto the mandrel of a decoiler when he suffered a serious crushing injury to his right upper arm. he was transported to loma linda university medical center, where he was hospitalized for about 29 hours.
an employee was repairing a bulk cooler-washer. he contacted energized conductors when he reached inside the control panel and was electrocuted.

at 2:15 p.m., employee #1, who worked for pacific stair company, was preparing to weld overhead clips on the "p-1" level of a building under construction. he fell about 30 ft., down an open vent shaft, striking a concrete surface. he was pronounced dead at the emergency room of harborview hospital.

at approximately 10:45 a.m. on march 13, 1998, employee #1 was found dead in the men's room at work. a syringe, a bottle of alcohol, and a vial of brown liquid were found with him. the coroner's report stated that the causes of death were early hepatic cirrhosis, multiple substance misuse, and hepatitis c. the employer 's accident prevention program specifically prohibited drug and alcohol use on the company's premises.
employee #1 was installing metal flashing on a tower when he collapsed and died of a heart attack.

at approximately 9:00 p.m. on september 24, 2001, employee #1, employed as woods railroad superintendent for weyerhaueser company, received a call from the plant site stating that one of the company's trains had derailed on the plant site a nd that his assistance was needed to respond to the situation. after employee #1 completed an initial survey of the situation at the plant site he went to the crew section bullpen to obtain equipment needed to get the train car back on the tracks. when employee #1 had not returned to the scene some 20 minutes later, the rail services manager went to find him to see if he needed help. the rail services manager found employee #1 in bullpen lying next to his truck. employee #1 was unresponsive and exhibited no vital signs following emergency first-aid/cpr. employee #1 was transported to a local hospital where he was pronounced dead as a result of a major heart attack.

employee #1 was operating a single-stroke, full-revolution clutch press and activated the two hand palm buttons. he reached into the press with his left hand to remove a part and the anti-repeat mechanism on the press malfunctioned. employee #1's fore-, middle, and ring fingers were amputated at the first knuckle.

employee #1 was standing on an a-frame ladder cutting a 10 ft section of a 4 in. steel pipe from an old heating system with a portable cutting torch. the pipe, which was 12 ft above the ground, fell on one side, hitting the corner of the window sill, then bouncing toward employee #1. the pipe knocked him off the ladder and he fell head first onto the concrete sidewalk. employee #1 was killed.
employee #1 fell 18 ft from the roof of a residence and was killed. the residence was not a job site.

at approximately 3:00 p.m. on august 18, 1994, employee #1 and three coworkers were attempting to lower a 10 inch diameter, 3 section drain pipe into a trench that measured 60 feet long by 4 1/2 feet wide by 12 feet deep. when the pipe was lowered into the trench by the excavator using a chain sling, the pipe came apart. a coworker went into the trench to connect the section and yelled for assistance. when another coworker went in to help, the wall collapsed around him. employee #1 jumped in the trench to dig him out. the wall collapsed further and completely buried employee #1. rescue personnel dug one worker out safely, but couldnot save employee #1, who died. another coworker escaped the cave-in by running in the opposite direction. no ladders, sloping, shoring, or trench box were in use, and the spoil bank was piled up on the edge of the trench.

a four-man crew was removing flue gas tubes from inside an air preheater using cutting torches. an air intake shaft was 7 ft from where employee #1 and a coworker were working. the supplied lighting was not bright enough to illuminate the open shaft. employee #1 was apparently moving an unused portable lamp toward the open shaft to protect it from falling debris when he stepped into the air shaft and fell 20 ft. by the time the fire department and others retrieved employee #1 from the bottom of the shaft, he was dead. the air preheater was considered a permit-required confined space. the opening to the air shaft at the 20 ft level was not guarded.

at approximately 9:30 a.m. on december 1, 1995, four employees were leveling the bottom of a sewer ditch that was approximately 120 ft long, 10 to 12 ft deep, a nd 5 to 6 ft wide. the side walls consisted of unstable soil. a track hoe was be ing used to clean the trench. the north wall of the trench collapsed, but only two employees were able to pull themselves out. employees #1 and #2 were buried in the trench. employee #1 was pronounced dead at the scene. employee #2 was transported to a local hospital and admitted.

at approximately 2:40 p.m. on august 31, 1995, employee #1 was installing sewer pipe in a trench when a wall of the trench collapsed and broke a 6 in. diameter steel gas pipe. he was struck in the head by a flying piece of lumber and killed .

at approximately 11:20 a.m. on or about may, 3, 1997, employee #1 and two coworkers were felling a tree. the tree was near a house, so the limbs were being removed before the tree was cut. one coworker was in the tree doing the cutting, and the other coworker was holding a rope attached to the limb to lower it after it was cut. one limb had already been removed and they were working on a second limb. as it was being lowered, the limb swung around and hit an approximately 3 in . diameter by 8 in. long dead branch. the dead branch flew about 20 ft and struck employee #1 in the face and head. the first coworker finished lowering the cutlimb and went to help employee #1, while the second coworker had the owner of the house call 911. the two coworkers tried to keep employee #1 sitting upright until ems arrived. employee #1 was killed.
an employee was painting the metal structure inside a substation. he contacted a 34-kilovolt energized part within the substation and was electrocuted.

on may 6, 1996, employee #1, a cleaning lady, was defrosting a refrigerator. she was chipping away ice from the freezer compartment with a knife when she punctured a gas line, releasing sulfur dioxide. employees #1 through #3 were taken to the hospital. employees #2 and #3 experienced coughing, sore throats, and diffic ulty breathing. all three were treated, released, and returned to work on may 7, 1996. the amount of sulfur dioxide was listed as 1.75 lb.

employee #1 was driving a truck east on old stage road in greenville, tn. he pulled his truck onto a railroad crossing and into the path of a norfolk southern train that was traveling at 32 mph. the train knocked his truck off the road intoan embankment. employee #1 was ejected through the driver's door, into the side of the moving train, and then thrown into a ditch a short distance along the tracks. he suffered critical head injuries and died in the hospital two weeks late r, never having regained full consciousness.

an employee was working from an aluminum ladder when he contacted a 277-volt conductor. he was electrocuted. a coworker received an electric shock and burns on his hands when he tried to remove the first employee from the ladder. the coworker was hospitalized for his injuries.

as part of his regular duties for fresh foods of ca, inc., employee #1 was attempting to clean carrots from under a belt conveyor that was installed at about a 36 degree angle to the floor, with the base about 6 in. above the floor. an upright member of the belt caught his gloved hand and pulled it through the conveyorhousing. his left hand sustained a compound 4th metacarpal displaced fracture. employee #1 was kept in the hospital overnight for observation.

at or about 10:40 a.m. on may 1, 1991, employee #1 was operating a delta unisawtable saw making end caps from a piece of laminated plastic 30 in. by 6 in. by 1 /16 in. thick. employee #1 was making a drop/plunge cut, which is considered a class b operation. employee #1 attempted to feed two pieces of slick plastic, one piece on top of the other piece, into the saw. in doing so, the pieces hung up between the guide and the table's cutting surface. he continued to hold on to the piece and his right finger was drawn into the moving saw blade.

at approximately 1:30 a.m. on november 14, 1997, employee #1, a was working as a machinist at gnb corporation, hayward, ca. his right thumb became caught by a rotating plastic shaving, which wrapped around it and pulled it in toward the rotating turret. when employee #1 attempted to pull away, the shaving tightened, am putating his thumb. no accident-related violation was found.

at approximately 7:45 a.m. on october 9, 1997, employee #1, a laborer for trinity properties, was working on a building in san francisco that reportedly had water leaks. after a rain, a group of employees went to the flat rooftop to inspectit and to locate the leak. upon completion, they were heading toward the fire escape when employee #1 tripped and fell through a skylight onto a concrete floor . he sustained head injuries and was taken to san francisco general hospital.

an electrician was installing a busway disconnect switch onto an energized 480-volt three-phase busway. although the supply side of the switch was energized, the secondary side was not because he had not yet inserted the fuses. the electric ian was using a metal allen wrench to tighten the load-side conductor lugs. the door to the busway switch enclosure was open and the operating handle was in the closed position, which would energize parts such as the line-side fuse-holders. he had connected the load-side phase conductors and was tightening the lug for the neutral conductor. the employee apparently left the allen wrench in the lug. as he turned around to adjust the position of the aerial lift from which he was working, the allen wrench apparently fell out of the lug and caused an electric al fault. the ensuing electric arc burned the employee. he was hospitalized for his injuries.

employee #1, a diver, was connecting a pump motor in a fish screen well. he exited the well using a 30 foot fixed ladder and stepped across the grate being used as a landing platform. within 10 seconds of exiting the well, he spoke briefly with a coworker, then collapsed and fell through the well's grated opening. the well, which measured 34 inches wide by 9 feet long and 30 feet deep, had differe nt sections of grating covering the well's opening. a 5 1/2 foot section of the grating was removed to allow material to be fed to the bottom of the well. no protection was provided to prevent employee #1 from stepping into the opening. he died of severe head, chest, and abdominal injuries.

at 11:45 a.m. on june 30, 1994, employee #1, a forklift operator, had just set a box of coiled twine on his forklift beside his seat. (all operators carry a boxof coiled twine to stabilize the top layer of cartons.) he was moving his forklift ahead slowly when he noticed that the end of the twine was hanging over the edge of his forklift. he grabbed the twine to pull it up; at the same time, the forklift's right rear wheel ran over the end of the twine. apparently his rightindex finger was caught in a loop of twine. it was severed at the first joint. employee #1 and his fingertip were taken to the ucla medical center. the staff treated his amputation, but was unable to reattach his fingertip.

employee #1, a senior engineer with over 20 years of experience, was using a stick-mounted spray can to mark the relocation points of railroad ties. he felt a twinge in his back, but continued to work that day. after undergoing non-invasive treatment for a number of weeks, he underwent day surgery on november 13, 1998 to relieve the pressure caused by compacted discs and a pinched nerve.

at 2:00 p.m. on july 14, 1995, employee #1, of feather river food, a company that processes and packs prunes, had climbed atop the stainless steel cover of a prune pitter machine (a conveyor system) to steam clean curtains above the machine . he slipped and fell 9 ft 5 in., sustaining a fractured pelvis and a hairline fracture to his left wrist. employee #1 was not using any fall protection and the cover was wet and slippery.

at 2:30 p.m. on june 26, 1995, employee #1 and his foreman were dumping chips out of the dump bed of an elevating boom truck that they were using during a treetrimming operation. employee #1 climbed onto the boom truck to raise the boom, which in turn would raise the dump bed. after the boom was raised, and before employee #1 descended, the foreman began to move the truck to spread the wood chips in the company's storage yard. employee #1 lost his balance and fell. his feet and ankles were run over by the truck's rear wheels. employee #1 sustained a large bump between his eyes, facial abrasions, abrasions and lacerations on his legs below the knees, fractures to both ankles, and crushed a foot.

employee #1 was part of a crew that was reroofing the roof over an indoor swimming pool at their employer's home. the newly-applied roof was about 12 ft high an d had 12 ft by 4 ft skylight openings. the skylights had been removed and the holes, which were about level with the roof, were covered with brown construction paper. employee #1 was tending hose while a coworker was spray painting. while tending hose near a skylight opening, employee #1 fell through the opening to the concrete next to the pool. his pelvis was fractured in two places. the employer , who operates a small roofing company, was cited for a serious violation of t8ccr 1632(e) for failing to guard or cover the skylight openings.

on august 13, 2001, employee #1 was operating a toledo full-revolution power press with two hand grips. employee #1 placed his hands in the die feed to remove scrap. as he removed scrap, the clutch rod broke. this caused the ram to continuerunning and ultimately crushing employee #1's small finger on his left hand. em

on or about june 22, 1995, iron from the cupola was being melted in the melting department of the dalton foundry and transferred by troughs to holding furnaces. the last section of these troughs was water-cooled and fed the holding furnace. iron was being put into the #2 holding furnace. at approximately 2:41 a.m., a hole developed in the water-cooled nozzle section of the trough system that fed iron into the #2 furnace. the water became entrapped under the iron, and at approximately 2:45 a.m. it created an explosion. this explosion caused iron to fly out of the furnace and start several fires. within 1 minute, another explosion occurred, sending more iron out of the furnace. some employees thought there wouldbe no more explosions and went into the work area to put out the fires. another, larger explosion occurred that sent out a large amount of molten iron. employees #1, #2, and #3 were killed. employees #4 through #17 were injured. four of the m were hospitalized.

employee #1 was fabricating metal parts on an amada press brake, adding various bends to the parts. he was holding a part in the point of operation and stepped on the pedal to cycle the machine. the part moved upward, trapping his left thumb against the brake body and amputating its tip. the machine was controlled by a single guarded pedal; the point of operation was unguarded.

employee #1 was cutting a trench for a sewer line through what the company class ified as solid rock. the rock had fissures and cracks and the excavation was on a known fault line. most of the trench was not more than 3 feet deep, but at the accident site, because of the shape of the hill that it was on, the trench was 7 1/2 feet deep. the trench was being cut by a diamond saw 2 feet wide. the bottom of the trench was 2 feet wide and the top of the trench was 32 inches. two large rocks separated from the south side of the trench. the first rock is estimated to have been 6 feet long, an average of 3 feet wide, and an average of 1 footthrough. the first rock landed on employee #1's head and pinned him between it and a cast iron pipe. the second rock was much larger. it sealed the top of the trench, but never came into contact with the first rock or with employee #1, who was alive when he was removed from the trench. he was transported to the dixie medical center, where he died about 2 hours later.
