at approximately 7:10 a.m. on may 15, 1997, employee #1, who worked at the usc medical center, was loading empty boxes into the compacting chamber of a kilkom baling press, model kxd48, serial #18835, patent #3,945,313. the compactor was us ed to compress empty boxes and to secure the bales of empty boxes with ties. employee #1 was loading in boxes when the machine's platen fell, striking almost all of his right arm, from near his shoulder to his fingertips. he sustained sever e injuries, and underwent immediate surgery to repair the damage and stop the bleeding. additional surgery was performed two days later. in an interview with employee #1 on september 15, 1997, he stated that he had been hospitalized for eight days following the accident. he did not lose his arm, but as of september he was still undergoing therapy. he could move his fingers slightly, but he was unable to make a fist. employee #1 was still wearing a bandage to keep the swelling down, and he was able to raise his right arm only slightly, and with difficulty . an inspection of the compactor revealed that its pin had cracked at precisely the halfway point, which released the platen and eliminated any chance of arrest ing its fall.

at approximately 6:30 p.m. on may 26, 1998, employee #1, who worked as a meat grinder machine operator, sustained a minor amputation of the flesh at the tip of the left index finger. the accident happened while reaching inside the grinder to grab a falling wrench. employee #1 had been operating the grinder for two years at this meat rendering, processing, and packaging facility. at the time of the accident, upon disconnecting the main electrical switch to the wyler grinder, employee #1 attempted to loosen the blade and plate assembly in order to replace it with one for a different-size grind. such a replacement is usually performed by the operators up to two times during the day. in this particular instance, employee #1's greasy hand slipped and the 15-lb wrench fell in the grinder. employee #1 reached for it and his left index finger hit the blade. as a result, a part of the flesh at the tip was amputated. employee #1 was hospitalized and treate d at us health works. there were no percipient witnesses to the accident. the em

plastopan north america, inc. is a plastic container manufacture. on july 9, 199 9, employee #1 was vacuuming and emptying plastic pellets inside the hopper. the re are seven heating zones outside the heating barrel of the battenfeld injection-molding machine. each heating temperature was set differently. heating zone number 1 is the nozzle heating zone, and the set temperature was 300 degree fahrenheit. zones two through 7 were the barrel heating zones, and their set temperatures were 350, 449, 449, 449, 339, and 325 degrees fahrenheit respectively. the set temperatures of each heating zone were monitored and controlled by thermocouples and controllers. a computer station monitors the setting temperature, pressures, and retention times, so the machine can run automatically. at the time of the accident, the thermocouple on heating zone number 7 was not functioning properly. usually, the heating temperature was set between 350 to 450 degree fahrenheit with a 65 degree fahrenheit plus or minus tolerance level. the actual temperature was 600 to 650 degrees fahrenheit. the temperature was too high, which caus ed the raw material to become a lump of molten plastic within the cylinder throat. some of the molten plastic inside the heating chamber was gasified, this created a pressure inside the barrel. when employee #1 was vacuuming out the pellets , the lump became disturbed and was removed. pressure inside the barrel released , and the molten plastic erupted and splashed on employee #1's face and arm. the thermocouple on heating zone number7 was defective and was not functioning properly. this caused heating zone number 7 to overheat, which resulted in this accident. the thermocouple should have been inspected and maintained on a regularly base, so the set temperature in the heating zone could not be overheated. the overheated conditions within heating zone number 7 caused the raw material to become a lump of plastic and jammed the cylinder throat. the material hopper could not slide pass the cylinder throat to the other opening. the raw material in the hopper could not be taken out. the employer required that employee #1 go to the top of the hopper to vacuum and empty the hopper. this placed employee #1 in a position of exposure to the molten plastic. molten plastic erupted and splashed onto employee #1's face and arm, and he was hospitalized with burns.

at approximately 11:55 a.m. on september 3, 1999, employee #1, a welder, asked a forklift teamster to remove a dumpster. the dumpster was located near the tool crib area. employee #1 asked the driver if he could ride on the forklift, so he could point out the location of the dumpster. he climbed on the forklift and satnext to the teamster's right side. employee #1 and the teamster proceeded to the tool crib. the dumpster was on the ground below the tool crib shipping door. the teamster turned to retrieve the dumpster. before he had come to a complete stop, employee #1 stepped off the forklift. the back right tire caught his right heel and trapped it. employee #1 suffered broken ankle, tibia, and fibula. he was hospitalized.

at approximately 6:55 a.m. on december 1, 1999, employee #1 was standing behind a bobtail truck helping the yard supervisor unload tilt-up pipe braces from a bobtail truck. the yard supervisor was operating a forklift. employee #1 rolled braces to the edge of the truck bed so that the forklift driver could easily pickup the braces. when the forklift driver raised the forklift past the gates of the truck, employee #1 was proceeding to roll other braces from left to right for the next pickup. while the forklift driver was backing up and making a turn, one brace fell off from the fork and struck employee #1's neck and upper back. employee #1 was taken to beverly hospital for medical treatment where he was hospital ized for one week. he was then transferred to presbyterian hospital for an additional two weeks. employee #1 sustained a fractured cervical bone and nerve damage.

at approximately 6:10 p.m. on december 2, 1999, an accident occurred in the rece iving department at the bimbo bakeries usa. at the time of the accident, employee #1, who was a maintenance engineer, was standing next to a flour bin unit and waiting for it to shut down. the bin filled with flour, toppled over, and fell on employee #1. employee #1 sustained severe spinal injuries and trauma to the right side of his body. he was transported to the los angeles county hospital.

at approximately 2:10 p.m. on december 30, 1999, an employee reached into a mach ine to remove a jammed piece of a flat corrugated sheet of an unknown material from a conveyor. the employee's right fourth finger was caught at the nip point of the lead pusher. he suffered an amputated right thumb at the distal phalanx.

at approximately 9:00 a.m. on july 26, 2000, employee #1 was driving a clark for klift, serial #c20p-275-509-662, when it tipped over. he became pinned underneath the upper carriage and suffered crushing chest injuries. employee #1 was kille d.

employee #1 was a day shift warehouseman with van rex, a warehouse storage and distribution center of gourmet foods. he was rotating boxes of bread on the top shelf of a shelving rack in the refrigerated storage area when he fell approximately 8 ft onto the cement floor. employee #1 sustained a fractured right clavicle and a fractured scapula. he was transported by ambulance to white memorial cent er, where he was treated and released several hours later. no fall protection was provided by the employer.

on february 26, 2002, employee #1 was assisting a coworker, who just took over the hose stripping duty for titan industries. titan industries is a manufacturer of industrial rubber hose. as part of the manufacturing procedures, compressed a ir is used to inflate rubber hoses so it can become separated from the steel man drel. a nylon strap strips the hose from the mandrel utilizing the conveyor syst em as the power source. this entire process is known as the hose stripping proce ht of the mandrel if it fell, but these bars were all located below the steel frame of the stripper and would not have being able to save the employee's finger. in addition, there were emergency stops along the entire workstation for immediate stop of the conveyor. the accident investigation determined that the holding devices used to secure the mandrel during the stripping operation were not adequately designed to prevent movement of the mandrel. the holding devices such as a single point clamp down vice or nylon strap were not able to securely hold the mandrel while the conveyor belt was put in a reverse direction.

at approximately 10:30 a.m. on november 27, 2001, employee #1, a line foreman of express cable communications inc., was working together with two coworkers in a residence home located in arcadia, california. they were pulling on a fiber rope that was stuck in a tree. employee #1 climbed up on a 4-ft wall to pull on the fiber rope when it broke loose, causing employee #1 to lose his balance and to fall approximately 4 ft. employee #1 sustained a fracture to the right hip. employee #1 was taken to arcadia hospital where he was treated for his injuries. he was not hospitalized. express cable communications inc. is primarily a communication contractor. employee #1 worked for the company for one day.

employee #1 was working on a roof approximately 23.3 ft above the ground. he was descending an aluminum extension ladder when his foot missed a rung and he lost his hand grip and fell to the ground. employee #1 suffered a lacerated chin and a fractured right knee and was hospitalized.

on october 11, 2002, employee #1, a field service representative of cox communic ations, fell off a ladder while servicing an overhead cable line. cox communications is a communications service, repair and provider. employee #1 was a non-con tract employee of cox communications. employee #1 was working alone on a 28-footwerner fiberglass extension ladder, model 7128 electro master, hooked to a mids pan wire. the midspan was measured to be 18-feet 5-inches above the dirt ground. employee #1's job was to replace a damaged drop line that runs to and services cable in san diego, california. this is a residential home and employee #1 was working on the overhead line across the street from this residential home. there was tension from the drop line attached to the span. employee #1 removed his positioning belt lanyard and then cut the span wire. employee #1 disconnected his belt lanyard, a bashlin industries number 1511n with an 81 inch leather lanyard, from the ladder and span because he anticipated descending the ladder immediately after cutting the drop line. employee #1 was on the ladder when the tension of the drop line released. this release of tension created a slingshot type action with employee #1 standing on and holding onto ladder. the ladder did not come unhooked from the span, but the slingshot action caused employee #1 to lose his grip and become propelled backwards. employee #1 fell 18-feet 5-inches to the ground. due to the fall, employee #1 received a laceration on his head, a collapsed lung, fractured 3 ribs and his right hand, and lost consciousness twice. employee #1 was hospitalized.

on march 4 2003, employee #1, of j & s glazing, was using a 40 ft jlg 400s arial lift truck to install windows at the site of a new two-story commercial building. the operator moved the bucket too close to the 12,000-volt overhead power line and it contacted one of the phase conductors. employee #1 received an electric shock and sustained burns to both arms, internal injuries, and damage to his eyesight.

at approximately 10:00 a.m. on october 13, 1997, employee #1 was changing the dimensions of inset shelves in the bedding department of a large retail department store. the shelf that he was changing was 9.67 ft from the ground. he was stand ing on the second or third step from the top and had one foot on the step and the other foot on the shelf. the shelf gave way; he tried to get full weight back on ladder but in the process pushed the ladder away from himself causing the fall. employee #1 hit either the ladder or a bedding floor fixture nearby. he sustained two broken vertebrae and laceration to head. he was transported to st. agne s hospital and released three days later. employee #1 stated he was kept in the hospital for observation only; making this accident non-reportable. the ladder was a relatively new 12 ft fiberglass stepladder and was in good condition. he stated that company trained him on ladder safety and employees are paired up for a "buddy system". at the time of the accident, the other employee had been holding the ladder, but left for a short time to get more supplies.

at approximately 9:00 p.m. on march 17, 2006, an employee worked for sun west fruit co., a fruit packing facility. the employee palletized fruit and worked for the company for two weeks. he entered the operation area of the machine and was picking up fruit. as he did this, the machine, which was in mid-cycle at the tim

at approximately 1:30 p.m. on november 26, 1996, employee #1 was working in a 6 ft deep excavation when it caved in. he was dug out in approximately 10 minutes. employee #1 was transported to the hospital, where he was examined and released 1 1/2 hours later, having sustained no serious injuries. the excavation had no cave-in protection.

on november 26, 1996, employee #1, of pride petroleum services, inc., bakersfield, ca, was working on rig #320 at bechtel well #315- 31s. the crew was killing the well, and employee #1 was operating the circulating pump. he equalized the circulating pump with the well pressure, the casing valve was opened, and they began pumping down. employee #1 looked at the pump pressure gauge and saw it rise suddenly to 2,200 psi. the relief valve operated, and fluid was discharged to the holding tank. suddenly, a 25 in. long section of 2 in. o-pipe with a clamp broke loose at the tank, striking employee #1 on the shoulder and ribs. he sustained fractured ribs.
employee #1 was sweeping up mushrooms from the floor when her head got too close
employee #1 was hospitalized with a broken leg.

employee #1 was removing a plow disc from a tractor. all bolts were removed, but the disc was not secured against falling. when the tractor moved forward the frame of the disc dropped on employee #1's foot. employee #1 was taken to the hosp ital and treated for lacerations and broken bones.

at 1:00 a.m. on december 29, 1997, employee #1 and the crew of golden state drilling, inc. rig #18 were in the process of moving the 450 pound flow ditch. two of the employees were discussing how to do the move. employee #1 decided to try to move his end of the ditch. the ditch started to move towards the sub-mat. employee #1 placed three fingers inside the casing at the end of the ditch. the ditch made contact with the sub-mat, and his right index finger was trapped betweenthe sub-mat and the ditch. the tip of the finger was amputated.

employee #1 and eight coworkers were lifting up a frame to be attached to the roof. one of the supports snapped and put all the weight of the frame to one side. employee #1 and eight coworkers held on to the frame long enough to get the crew out of the way. employee #1 slipped while moving out of the way and was hit by frame in the shoulder. he was treated and released from a hospital after observation.

employee #1 received the task of assisting another coworker in emptying trash cans off located on the golf course. while the coworker was driving the flatbed go lf cart, employee #1 was holding a rope wrapped around the cans. the rope end was dangling and it wrapped around the wheel. the rope became taught, snapping employee's #1 thumb off.

at approximately 9:00 a.m. on february 16, 1999, a forklift operator was moving floor pallets with goods stacked 5 ft high on the pallet. the operator lifted a loaded pallet and backed up. he then pulled forward and struck employee #1 with the loaded pallet as she was walking by. she sustained bruises to her left leg, left arm, and neck. she was taken to the hospital for treatment and released four hours later. the forklift operator stated he could not see employee #1 over the loaded pallet.

during re-roofing operations of a light-industrial warehouse employee #1 was carrying a 8 ft by 4 ft piece of foam insulation which may have been caught in the wind. employee #1 was thrown backwards and fell through an unguarded skylight opening and fell approximately 36 ft to the concrete floor below. employee #1 was killed.

employee #1 was injured while grinding burrs off of a metal punch. employee #1 had rested his left hand on the tool rest of a grinder and was turning the punch with his right hand. employee #1's left hand was pulled into the grinder wheel. employee #1 let the punch contact the edge of the grinder wheel that pulled on the punch. employee #1's little finger on his left hand was amputated when it was pulled into the grinding wheel.

at approximately 6:10 a.m. on august 6, 2003, employee #1 was working at the evaporator-drum filler staging area. employee #1 was standing on a one-step platform unloading top boxes from a pallet for the drum operator. as employee #1 reached up to remove a 40-lb box of empty bags from the stacked pallet which had beenplaced on the roller conveyor with a forklift, employee #1 lost his balance and fell backward a distance of approximately 2 ft. employee #1 suffered a fracture when he hit his head on the cement wall and floor area. employee #1 was hospital ized.
on june 26, 2006, employee #1 was removing a stick from the stile rip feeder. he sustained an amputation to the tip of his left middle finger and was hospitalized.

on march 9, 2007, sodium hypochlorite was delivered into a tank of sulfuric acid , creating a chemical cloud. employee's # 1, #2, #3, #4, and # 5 were exposed to the cloud and taken to the hospital. they were treated for chemical inhalation and released.

on august 19, 1997, employee #1 was working at the mid-level of a scaffold erected around a tunnel input gate. this gate valve was approximately 20 ft wide and 20 ft high. employee #1 was engaged in routine scheduled maintenance. employee # 1 had ascended to the upper level of the scaffold to retrieve two bundles of zinc anodes. these are zinc rods were about 39-in. long and about 0.75-in. in diameter, and came packaged twelve to a bundle. each bundle weighed approximately twenty-five pounds. employee #1 attempted to climb down to his work area while carrying the two bundles of anodes. employee #1 lost his grip and fell about 18 ft to a concrete deck. employee #1 rolled on the deck and then fell 20 ft into the gate well.

on march 14, 2000,an accident occurred at a private residence being worked at by a labor ready employee. while unloading boxes from a u-haul trailer, employee # 1 fell while going down the loading ramp. he did not report to his supervisor that he was injured, and in fact told the other employees there that he was all right. later that evening he went to the emergency room complaining of back pain. he was admitted for observation, and mri's were performed which were negative.
below, a distance of about 6 feet. his injuries required hospitalization.

at approximately 11:05 a.m. on december 21, 2011 employee #1 of all fab precision sheetmetal suffered broken ribs when he fell from a forklift and landed on his side. at the time of the accident, employee #1 and employee #2 (forklift driver /operator), were moving an assembled frame to the paint shop adjacent to the site. employee #1 was inside the frame on top of a pallet, holding and balancing the frame on its sides, while employee #2 drove the forklift. the frame tipped over when the forklift hit an uneven surface as it was leaving the site's parking lot. employee #1 fell and the left side of his body hit the inside of the frame. as a result, employee #1 suffered a collapsed lung and multiple left rib fractures, according to the medical report. employee #1 was taken to santa clara valley medical center where he was evaluated, treated and admitted.

at about 3:25 p.m. on june 21, 2012, employee #1, a meat cutter with safeway inc ., was cutting beef shanks into slices when his index and middle fingers on his right hand came into contact with the running saw blade. as a result, employee # 1 suffered a partial amputation of these fingers. he was hospitalized for 12 days. he underwent surgery to reattach his fingers. employee #1 stated that he did not use a pusher plate when he was cutting beef shank on the band saw because he was not taught to use it for this operation, for slicing shanks. the employer provided signed documents by employee #1 (on the band saw safety sheet) which included a statement "you must use the pusher plate when cutting close to the product to prevent your fingers from getting too close to the blade." however, employee #1 never used the pusher plate and was not trained to use it. employee #1's supervisor (the current meat manager) admitted that he had observed employee #1 cut beef shanks in the past without using a pusher-plate on the band-saw it was determined that the employer failed to: implement safety training (t8 ccr 4543(c) ); and, ensure that its employee used the pusher plate provided with the hobart meat band saw (model 6801),when slicing short ends of meat.

at approximately 1:00 a.m. on january 13, 2003, employee #1 was working for an employer that produced stretch film and custom poly bags for commercial purposes. the employee was working alone, reprocessing scrap material by running it through a grinder/extruder machine. the machine was not working properly and the employee turned the power off. he observed some plastic near the motor's unguarded v -belt. while the belt was turning, the employee reached in to remove the plastic . his right hand was caught by the moving belt. the employee suffered an amputation of his right ring finger and fractures to his index and middle fingers. he was transported to st. joseph of orange hospital for treatment.

on march 15, 2006, a temporary employee was working in the assembly department of southern california plastics. the company was an injection-molding manufacture r of plastic specialty tools and other products. the employee was directed by he r immediate supervisor to operate a rother machine tools pneumatic press, serial number 12vsg/a1974. the machine was used to crimp rivets, about 0.5 inch in diameter, to each end of a length of twisted wire rope about 12 in. long. once ther e was a rivet sitting loosely on each end of the rope, they were placed in the pneumatic press, held in place by the operator, and crimped. a foot pedal activated the stamping action. the machine did not have a guard to keep the operator'sfingers out of the point of operation. upon placing the third or fourth assemblage into the point of operation for crimping, the employee activated the ram with the foot pedal. the ram came down on the employee's left long finger, which was still in the point of operation. the ram crushed the end of her finger, just above the first joint. she pulled her finger out, tearing off skin and flesh. she yelled for help, and coworkers came to her aid. she was taken to the company's walk-in clinic for medical treatment. the employee lost the tip of her left middle finger.

on august 18, 1999, employee #1 was operating a hyster forklift. while driving the forklift, he proceeded around a corner at an excessive speed causing the vehicle to overturn. he fell out of the vehicle and its back tire ran over his left foot crushing it. emergency medical personnel were summoned and took him to the hospital. he was hospitalized for two weeks and underwent orthopedic surgery to repair the bone, ligament, tendons, and muscle damage in his foot.

at approximately 4:20 p.m. on september 30, 1999, employee #1 suffered an amputation of the little finger of his left hand when he got his fingertip caught between the drive pulley and the v-belt of a canton-44 shear machine during repair/maintenance. employee #1 had manually pulled the triple v-belt connecting the flywheel and the drive pulley but did not let the belts go in time and sustained the amputation. employee #1 was hospitalized.

at approximately 9:00 p.m. on january 25, 2001, employee #1 amputated his middle finger while operating a saw to cut a piece of metal. his employer notified the division on february 1, 2001. employee #1 was using a metal cutting saw. the model number was nf-1214 t10. the piece of metal was measured at 6 feet by 3 feetby 2 inches. after trimming the sides of the piece, he was ready to make a first cut. he set the back gauge in forward motion. he pressed on the material to keep the material snug with the back gauge. as the material was moving forward, the glove on employee #1's left hand became caught between the moving part (material) and a groove (about 3 inches long, 2.5 inches wide, and 2 inches deep) on the table of the saw. he pulled his middle finger in to the pinch point. his middle finger then became amputated. after the incident, employee #1 was taken to kais er permanente hospital by another employee.

e direction to create a pinch point. the employee was wearing latex gloves on his hands while operating the grinding machine. while grinding the first part, he went too close to the pinch point to check the size of the part and the loose part of the glove on his left hand got pulled into the pinch point. his left middle finger got crushed in the pinch point. the accident resulted in amputation of his left middle finger and a severe laceration on his index and ring finger. the employee was transported to arrowhead regional medical center and was hospitalized for 2 days. the employer, parco inc., manufactures rubber seals.

at approximately 8:30 a.m. on september 25, 2002, employee #1, a machine operator, was adjusting a limit switch at the bottom of a concrete block unstacker, when a finger car with a full load of concrete blocks came from behind and pinned his legs between the lower base of the block unstacker and the lower base of the moving car. both his legs were fractured below the knees and he was hospitalized . further investigation revealed that employee #1 did not follow the employer'slockout tagout procedures. the employer was in the business of manufacturing concrete blocks. employee #1 was a regular machine operator and was training to be a foreman.

nal medical clinic, san bernardino, ca. then he was referred to the loma linda university medical center, loma linda, ca where his right index finger was amputated. the employee was discharged the same day.

on january 21, 1999, employee #1 was removing a steel grating cover (dimension of approximately 4 ft long by 3 ft wide by 1.5 in thick and weight of about 50 lb ) from the top of a sump pump. he placed his fingers into the grates and lifted the covering with his left hand. as he lifted the covering, he placed his righthand on the base of the grate opening and used his two hands to pry the grate covering off. as he separated the grating cover from its opening, it slipped out of his left hand and dropped on his right hand. the impact of the grate on his hand amputated the tip of his right middle finger.

at 9:00 a.m. on may 3, 2001, an accident occurred at a three-level residential construction site in hermosa beach, california. employee #1, a laborer, while using the staircase located on the west side of the building, died due to multiple injuries received from a fall through the 5-foot wide by 6-foot 10-inches high open side of the unguarded stairway landing, located 12-feet 10-inches above the asphalt street. at the time of the incident, employee #1 was part of a three-mancrew, carrying boxes containing drywall compound material to the third floor of the dwelling under construction. the employer, a drywall subcontractor, was contracted by the general contractor to complete all the interior dry wall components of a two story single family dwelling. it was found that the stairway landing was never guarded. on the day of the accident, employee #1 along with the lead person and another coworker drove up to the construction site to deliver 66 cart ons of drywall compound material and to clean up trash and debris piled up fromthe previous days work. as planned, employee #1 and a coworker started out by taking the boxes of drywall compound material from the company truck using a wheelbarrow up to the first floor garage level. meanwhile, the lead person cleared up space in a kitchen next to the staircase on the third level. employee #1 and the coworker hand-carried two boxes of drywall compound at a time using the staircase and placed them on the kitchen floor. by approximately 9:00 a.m., employee # 1 and the coworker had made several trips up and down the staircase and had placed 20 boxes. right after this, as employee #1 was coming down the staircase fromthe third floor to the ground floor to get more boxes and following behind the lead person, employee #1 fell out and off the exterior unguarded opening of the second floor landing, hitting the scaffolding planks outside on the exterior street below. the lead person, at the bottom of the staircase, heard the impact of his fall and climbed up the stairs to the first landing and looked outside and saw employee #1 laying on the street below on his back. the lead person and the coworker both ran immediately out to help employee #1 and found him profusely bleeding from his head. the lead person called the owner, informing him about the a ccident. the owner called the office and told them to call emergency medical services and rushed to the site. hermosa beach paramedics arrived at the site at 9: 15 a.m. employee #1 was given emergency treatment and then transported to the little company of mary hospital by their ambulance . the massive head injuries sustained by employee #1 in the fall were sufficiently severe to cause death withinapproximately half an hour of the incident. employee #1 was pronounced dead at the hospital at 9:30 a.m.

on february 25, 2006, employee #1, working for a steel fabricator, was painting a steel building element that was 30 ft by 16-in. by 40-in., and weighed 3,300 pounds. it was lying flat, supported by 4 steel sawhorses and spanned by pieces of wood, 4 ft by 4 ft. he needed to turn it over and to do so he hooked a chain sling to one side and began to raise it from flat to vertical. the sling was tipped with an open throated eye hoist hook that did not have a safety latch on it. as the assembly reached the vertical position, it began to wobble and simultaneously one of the 4x4's broke and the sling came off. it fell and caught employee #1 between it and another frame, crushing his head, killing him.
on july 29, 2002, employee #1 was killed when he was struck and run over by a forklift.

on december 6, 2005, employee #1 was operating a mobile compaction side loader garbage truck, number 8219. he exited the cab with the intent of clearing trash from the top of the truck. he climbed up the right side of the truck, while the c

at approximately 10:00 a.m. on august 1, 2001, employee #1, a day laborer for up c hauling company, was assigned to cut a large, heavy tree stump at a hillside site in san francisco, california. employee #1 was standing at an angle and was using a chain saw to cut the stump when the stump suddenly shifted in the wrong direction and fell toward him. it knocked him down and crushed his left arm against an adjacent shorter stump. employee #1's arm was almost completely severed by the blow. he was transported to san francisco general hospital, where he was admited for treatment. employee #1 had been hired on the day of the accident and was neither trained nor instructed in the hazards involved in the job assignment. it was recommended that the employer provide safety training that specifically addressed all identified jobsite hazards.

at approximately 3:45 a.m. on june 24, 2009, employee #1, a janitor, was pressure washing the patio on the outside of the metreon mall adjacent to the yerba buena gardens. the pressure washing unit consisted of a gasoline-powered pressure washer, an all american pressure washer, model number eh4030hdoe, mounted on a wheeled cart. the pressure washing unit was connected to an ordinary garden hose. the water from the hose was heated in a diesel-powered kettle and then sent through a rubber hose to a triggered spray gun at a rate of 4 gpm and 3,000 psi. employee #1 power washed the patio for 2.5 hours without incident. as he was standing 8 feet away from the pressure-washing unit, the rubber hose connecting the washer gun to the main body of the pressure washing unit burst, spraying employee #1 with a high-pressure jet of hot water. he was taken to the hospital, where he was treated for second degree burns on the right side of his torso, right buttocks, and right arm and was released after five hours.

at approximately 6:15 a.m., employee #1 was assisting in a saw blade change on a gang edger. employee #1, a head filer, was visiting the gang edger to assess the cutting blades functions and began to assist another employee in a blade chang e by blowing down the in-feed of the edger. the other employee locked out the electrical power to the edger, and employee #1 raised the main feed roller, which was controlled by pneumatics, using a small valve located on the front panel of the edger. employee #1 reached into the machine with his right arm holding a short 7.75-in. air tool and began to blow down the sawdust. employee #1's left hand was on the control valve which he accidentally activated, dropping the in-feed roller on his right forearm. employee #1 suffered a crushed ulna and radius of the right arm. employee #1 was hospitalized for his injuries.

employee #1, an equipment setup worker employed by mid willamette lumber inc., a planing and moulding mill, located in scio, or, was setting up a woods 132 gm moulding machine. the machine was locked out by the employee during the initial setup procedures. he then removed the locks and energized the machine. wood was fed into the machine by another worker and the product was checked for dimension by employee #1. employee #1 then returned to the side of the moulding machine an d noticed that the rear wood guide was loose. he reached across the machine to tighten a loose nut with a wrench. while tightening the nut, the wrench slipped a nd his right hand struck the exposed spindle of the cutting head, which rotates at 7000 rpm. the spindle broke three fingers on his right hand. first aid was applied and employee #1 was transported to salem memorial hospital for medical care.

employee #1 was standing near a running band saw wiping his hands with a rag. as employee #1 tossed the rag toward a bench, the exposed portion of the band saw blade caught the rag and pulled employee #1's hand toward it, amputating the endof his index finger. employee #1 was hospitalized.

on november 30, 2009, employee #1 was found lying near a snowplow on the truck he was operating. a pry bar was found lying near him. it is thought that he was using the bar on the spring loaded plow when it kicked back and struck him in the head. employee #1 was dead.

at approximately 9:43 a.m. on april 3, 2009, employee #1 was operating a heidelberg harris printing press (model number printstream-100, serial number om3399-6) . employee #1's job was to help the press man operate the printing press. accord ing to employee #1, the paper broke from its continuing roll because it was the soft type of paper. employee #1's supervisor stopped the press and spliced the paper then he put the machine on "inch" mode so that the paper roll could web its elf through the rollers. the paper roll began to go backwards and started unrave ling quickly. employee #1 placed his right hand near the roll and the paper wher e it was going backwards in order to stop it in place. employee #1 stated he could not tell if the roll was moving or not. the roll pulled employee #1's hand be tween the rollers and squeezed his hand through the half-inch opening. employee #1 tried to pull his hand back but could not. employee #1 felt his hand getting crushed. then, employee #1's hand went through the rollers but part of his palmwas still stuck between the rollers and the rollers started tearing his skin off of his right hand. employee #1 tried to turn the machine off with the emergencybutton but his right hand was caught in the machine and the emergency stop button was on the right side of the machine. employee #1 tried to swing his foot or left hand to turn the machine off. the machine finally turned off. once the mach ine was stopped, employee #1 still could not get his hand out from between the rollers since the opening was still only half an inch. employee #1's hand was a lot thicker than half an inch. employee #1 told coworkers to remove the top rolle r with a wrench by loosening it to free his hand. a coworker did remove the roller, and they freed employee #1's hand. employee #1 hand was badly damaged. the remaining skin on employee #1's hand was just hanging off; it was almost a comple te degloving. a coworker called emergency services, and the ambulance took employee #1 to the hospital. the medical staff gave employee #1 morphine because he was in so much pain. that same evening at approximately 8:00 p.m., the doctors performed surgery on employee #1's hand. employee #1 was hospitalized.

at approximately 12:20 p.m. on december 22, 2010, employee #1 was working at a facility of a firm that manufactured rubber and plastic hoses and belts. employee #1 had been employed at the facility since august 10, 2010, and he had been with the corporation since july 1, 1984. employee #1 was on the roof to investigate the origin of a water leak from a chiller. the employer had three other employees who went up onto the roof on a regular basis to check equipment and conduct other inspection checks. employee #1 was a plant manager and would not normally have gone up to the roof to view the leaking chiller. the supervisor of maintenance, however, wanted to show employee #1 where the leak was coming from and how they were going to fix it. the supervisor of maintenance stated that he did not see if employee #1 slipped. he did see employee #1 lose his balance while near the skylight and fall through. the supervisor of maintenance was the only one accompanying employee #1 while he was on the roof. employee #1 fell through a skylight made of an acrylic plastic dome. it was not equipped with a skylight guard or standard railing on all four sides. he fell 23 feet. employee #1 was rushed to the hospital, but he was pronounced dead at approximately 3:00 p.m. according to the medical examiner's report, employee #1 sustained multiple skull fractures, diffused global cerebral edema, a subarachnoid hemorrhage, extra-axial hemorrhage, pneumorrhachis, multiple facial fractures, multiple skull base fractures, multiple fractured ribs, left pneumothorax, fractured scapula, fractured pelvis, pulmonary contusions, and multiple thoracic and lumbar spine fractures. multiple traumatic injuries resulting from the fall were listed as the cause of death.

a power line worker climbed a utility pole to replace a broken insulator on a 40 00-volt overhead power line. the employee had rubber insulating gloves in his possession but had not yet donned them. as he was positioning himself on the pole, he simultaneously contacted the crossarm brace and pole the ground. he received an electric shock and fell from the pole. he died either from the electric shock or from injuries sustained in the fall. a 4000-volt phase conductor had been lying directly on top of the crossarm. the voltage on the metal brace was measure d at 2340 volts during the investigation of the accident. the pole ground was discolored, and the adjacent wood showed burn marks where the worker had placed on e of his hands.

employee #1 was standing on the third floor base plate attempting to move a large roof truss when he fell 27 ft to the ground. the truss followed employee #1 do wn and struck him in the head. employee #1 died.

on february 11, 2005, an employee, a nephew of the proprietor of the business that deals in obtaining, repairing and selling automobiles, was setting up to dropa transmission from a 1991 chrysler, dodge caravan, when the vehicle rolled of its jack stands and crushed him underneath. the employee was killed.

employee #1 was working in the pit of the #3 sms hydraulic extruder, removing scrap aluminum from a pocket under the loader, while a signalman relayed messages from employee #1 to the press operator about when to raise and lower the loader. as he entered the rear of the pit, employee #1 told the signalman to raise the loader, and the signalman shouted the instruction to the operator. not seeing that the cycle stop button was pushed in, the signalman shouted and gestured to the operator to push in the stops. turning to see what employee #1 was doing, the signalman watched as the loader fell on him. the signalman then turned again and shouted to the operator to raise the loader. however, because of the high ambient noise and a miscommunication of hand signals, the loader arm was lowered ontoemployee #1. he was transported by paramedics to the local hospital, where he was pronounced dead.

on june 13, 2007, employee #1 and a coworker, working for a drywall contractor, were working in a small office. they were using tuff bond adhesive to install stick pins for insulation on the walls. employee #1 was operating a scissor lift a nd was exiting the room by driving the lift through a doorway. as he exited the room, the lift surged forward and he struck the wall with his chest. he was able to back the lift away and exit the room. he told the coworker that he was thirs ty and was given water. when he bent down to take a drink, he became dizzy. the coworker helped him out of the lift and laid him in the floor. employee #1 was transported to a hospital and died.

on june 15, 2011, employee #1, a 41-year-old male, mechanic working at bak autoinc., was working on a vehicle that was on a jack stand. the vehicle collapsed onto employee #1 and crushed his upper torso. employee #1 was pronounced dead at the scene by dekalb county emergency medical personnel.

ther to a heart attack or cyanide poisoning. the investigation revealed no chemical odor in the restroom, on employee #1, or in the surrounding area; however, a hazmat team found a few ounces of a cyanide chemical inside a bucket in the restroom.

on december 14, 2008, employee #1 was opening a drain valve on a vessel to remove any water that had settled to the bottom of the vessel. the vessel contained a mixture of 80 percent ethane and 20 percent propane. when he opened the valve to drain the water into and enclosed plastic tank an explosion occurred. a fire burned the ethane and propane mixture through the open drain valve. employee #1 was thrown from the area by the explosion, receiving a broken arm, dislocated shoulder and other injuries. employee #1 was hospitalized.

at approximately 12:30 a.m. employee #1 was found dead in the "feather whipper" of this chicken parts rendering plant. it is not known whether the deceased fellinto the "feather whipper" or entered purposely through the opening on top of the "feather whipper". employees routinely walk on top of the "feather whipper" where the opening is located to monitor the amount of feathers in the adjacent "feather hopper" and to drain the lung tank. the lid or cover for the opening did not fasten in place. the "feather whipper" consists of two horizontal shafts, each having metal bars welded to them. these metal bars separate the matted feathers. employee #1 died due to multiple trauma to the lower body caused by the metal bars of the "feather whipper".

at approximately 9:30 a.m. on may 2, 2000, employees #1 and #2 were laying sewer pipe in the south end of a 7.4 ft to 9.6 ft deep, 4.5 ft to 5 ft wide, and more than 50 ft long trench when the east wall caved in. they were buried and died of asphyxia. the trench had no form of cave-in protection.

on july 19, 2002, employee #1 and two coworkers were installing shingles on the roof of a new residence under construction when it began to storm. they rushed to finish the capping and then began to descend the ladder. one coworker came down successfully. employee #1 was beginning to climb down when he slipped and fell 30 ft off the ladder to the ground. employee #1 was killed.

on november 14, 2008, employee #1 was riding on the passenger side rider's stepon the rear of a mack refuse truck, model number ctp713b. a coworker was driving the truck. the coworker saw employee #1 get onto the riding step and grasp the handle with both hands. he then drove the vehicle at approximately 10 miles per hour for approximately 400 feet when he noticed that employee #1 was no longer standing on the riding step. the coworker stopped the vehicle, got out, and found employee #1 lying on the ground. employee #1 was transported by emergency services to the hospital, where he underwent surgery for a blood clot. further invest igation revealed that employee #1 may have slipped off the truck due to the wetsurface of the step.

employee #1 was working for a firm that transported automobiles by way of railroad car carriers. unlike boxcars, car carriers were not loaded from the side. doors at each end opened, so that an entire string of car carriers could be loaded at one time. bridge plates were used to bridge the gaps between car carriers and allow automobiles to be driven from one car carrier to the next. employee #1 was last seen by a coworker putting a bridge plate between two car carriers so that automobiles could be driven across the gap. several minutes later, the same or another coworker found employee #1 on the ground unconscious. employee #1 was taken to a hospital, where he later died. an autopsy determined that employee #1 had died from multiple injuries sustained from a fall from a car carrier.

employee #1 and #2 were installing exterior sheet rock board and working at the height of 30-ft when their man lift tipped over. the lift was equipped with a self-leveling feature allowing the wheels to adjust to uneven ground. the employees were hospitalized and treated for fractures. the cause was unknown at the timeof this report.

a crew was setting a modular home onto a basement foundation. employee #1 was removing the slats that held the plastic wrap. he sat or stood on the basement foundation and fell into basement. he suffered minor head injuries but was not hosp italized.

a crew was laying a roof deck and insulation. the crew had laid three sheets of decking out beyond the leading edge to be used as a work platform to assist in rolling out the insulation. on the day of the accident the winds were extremely high. there were two workers on this make-shift platform on the day and at the time of the accident. employee #1 was going to step across from the platform to the deck to assist with stretching and securing the insulation. as he was stepping across, he fell 27 feet to the pavement and was killed. employee #1 was not wearing his personal fall arrest system and there was no other fall protection syst em available.

while operating a drum-type hydraulic compacting machine, the employee disconnected his seat belt to light a cigarette. when the machine hit a rut, the employeelost his balance and fell out of the seat and in front of machine. the employeewas run over and fatally crushed.
employee #1 attempted to unblock a machine without de-energizing the circuitry. employee #1's hand was caught in the machine. employee #1 was hospitalized for lacerations to his hand.

on february 12, 1998, employee #1, a sole proprietor, was fatally injured when he apparently fell into a mobile cattle feeder that was equipped with an auger driven by a tractor power take-off. the feeder was manufactured by case schwartz. the feeder was used to process silage, and to place it into feed troughs by means of a chute attached to the unit. the feeder was not self-propelled, but was pulled by a tractor. it was equipped with a non-motorized auger that is powered by means of the power take-off unit of the tractor that tows it. employee #1 was working alone; there were no witnesses to the accident. however, based on an eval uation of the scene, employee #1 climbed up onto the feeder and accidentally fell in. why employee #1 climbed up onto the feeder, and how he fell into the unitcould not be determined.

employee #1 was assisting a coworker with trouble shooting grinder number two an d cleaning of sawdust build-up in the room. an explosion occurred in the west a baghouse and continued to grinder number two, resulting in serious burns to employee #1 who was hospitalized.

employee #1 was attempting to descend a ladder during an emergency evacuation exercise using a technique called an emergency ladder bail, which requires an individual to mount the ladder in a head first position. employee #1 lost his grip a nd fell approximately 18 ft to the ground head first and died.

employee #1, a maintenance worker, had been an employee for 6 years. the piece of equipment had been in place for 3 to 4 years. employee #1 was adjusting the to rtellini machine to regulate the amount of filler injected into each piece of pasta. employee #1 had the top cover removed and the machine was off. employee #1 made the adjustment and asked the operator to start the machine to test the adjustment. employee #1 had his hand on the top edge of the vertical side of the machine, as he leaned to his right to look at the front of the machine. as he leane d, his hand slid along the top edge into the rotating gears, amputating his left index finger at the first joint. employee #1 was hospitalized for treatment.

on july 25, 2005, employee #1, a construction worker, was working from a 24-footfiberglass extension ladder, which was propped up against the third-floor railing of a building. the ladder was not secured to the railing and slipped, causing employee #1 to fall about 20 feet to a concrete walkway. employee #1 was hospitalized and treated for a fractured finger and dislodging hardware, from a previous injury, in his ankle.

at approximately 7:00 a.m. on september 9, 2010, employee #1 was unloading trees and shrubs from the back of a truck. he carried them to a staging storage area approximately 20 ft, and he collapsed. the temperature was 55 degrees. employee #1 died of a heart attack.

on september 21, 2011, employee #1 of j.p. landscape, inc. received heat illness stress in connection to his employment as a crew leader. employee #1 was in charge of a small crew laying sod and plantings at a new eight-plex when he felt the symptoms. he stayed in the shade and drank water to soothe himself. after returning home his wife drove him to st. agnes medical center where he was admitted for treatment that evening. the er called the division upon discovery of the incident the next day. an investigation was conducted by the fresno dosh office.

at 10:00 a.m. on march 9, 2012, employee #1 was in the course of his normal workduties as an irrigation system installation foreman. the employee helped another employee connect piping. when he knelt down employee #1's knee was punctured by an unknown object in the dirt. employee #1 started having problems nine days later when his knee started turning red and swollen. the employee reported the in jury to his employer on march 19, 2012, when the infection got worse employee #1 was admitted to saint agnes medical center for treatment.

on april 20, 2012, employee #1 was working as an automotive dismantler at fresnotruck wrecking, inc. the employee was unloading a ford ranger truck to remove parts from it. employee #1 was unfastening the chain used to secure the parts to the forklift. the employee placed his right thumb between the tine of the forklift and chain. as the chain was released, its movement snared and crushed employee #1's thumb against the tine. the employee's right thumb was amputated at the tip. employee #1was sent to concentra occupational medical center, where the employee was treated and released.

on november 29, 2012, employee #1, a 27-year-old male, employee #2, a 26-year-old male, employee #3, a 44-year-old female employee #4, a 45-year-old female, employee #5, a 53-year-old female, employee #6, a 54-year-old female, employee #7, a 41-year-old male, employee #8, a 57-year-old male, employee #9, a 65-year-oldfemale, employee #10, a 59 -year-old female, employee #11, a 41-year-old female, employee #12, a 51-year-old female, employee #13, a 54-year-old female, employee #14, a 49-year-old female, employee #15, a 42-year-old female and employee # 1 6, a 45-year-old female with porterville unified school district were involved in a catastrophic event causing the sixteen employees to be transported to threemedical facilities after the release of three hundred pounds of r-507 refrigerant was released into porterville unified school district's central kitchen. the release occurred in the kitchen 47,000 cu. ft. cold room when the facility was no t occupied. at first, employee's entering the cold room did not detect or notice anything wrong, however as the refrigerant migrated from the cold room into the warmer cooking area, it expanded and filled the kitchen. employee's in these warmer areas were able to detect a sweet smell and then began experiencing upper respiratory and eye irritations, which triggered an evacuation of the facility. the refrigerant release was found to be the failure of the air handler's evaporator coil that was abraded by an electrical conduit rubbing against it. during the installation of the air handler a hold-down clip that secures the conduit was not installed and the defect occurred under a cover that normally would not be removed for normal maintenance.

at 5:00 a.m. on september 23, 1998, employee #1, an equipment operator, received a serious injury while working for federal express. employee #1 was pushing a mldlamd container on static rack #17 from the south to the north, with three other employees. the container became stuck and difficult to move near a canopy stanchion. employee #1 attempted to step around to the inside of the stanchion whenher foot slipped off the catwalk and down between conveyor rollers. the containe r was still being pushed by another worker who did not know she had slipped, pushing the container over her right leg, breaking it in two places. employee #1 was hospitalized to treat her injuries.

employee #1 developed an inguinal hernia while lifting a forklift wheel. he was lying in a twisted position on the floor when he lifted the wheel with one hand and held an axle in the other hand. he felt a sensation in his leg as if he had rolled over a tool when he lifted the wheel. initially, his injury was not serious, and he did not receive any medical treatment for his injury. however, surger y was later found necessary when the employee began to experience more pain. employee #1 was trained on proper lifting techniques prior to the incident.

on november 3, 2004, employee #1, a supervisor, was climbing an extension ladder to access the roof level of a mechanical room. the employee did not secure the ladder to prevent displacement. the ladder slipped outward and the employee fell 10 feet and landed on the ground. employee #1 sustained injuries that required surgery. the employee remains hospitalized.
on october 22, 2008, employee #1 slipped and fell at grade level of the floor. employee #1 was taken to the hospital and treated for her injuries.

on june 2, 2009, employee #1 was setting up a container trailer to hook up to the tractor. he was plugging a reefer cord on the chassis as the truck was backing up. employee #1 was knocked down on the rear end of the truck while the truck continued to back up. he became caught under the steel container where his chest was caught by the "kingpin" of the steel container, fracturing his ribs. he was able to free himself. he was hospitalized at highland hospital for treatment of six fractured ribs.

on january 5, 2010, a flight instructor, employee #1, and four of his coworkers were taking advantage of their staff flight-time. staff flight-time is a company benefit that allows instructors of the company to fly after their shift. the five workers were in the wind tunnel, with the wind flowing at top speed, 170 mph. all five workers flew to the top of the wind chamber, about 45-ft up. four of the workers flew down, but employee #1 was at the top of the wind tunnel hanging onto a stationary airfoil vane. the vibration sensor detected a fault, slowly shutting down one of the fans. employee #1 continued to hang onto the structure while the other four workers tried to restart the fan, to blow air into the tunnel , to enable employee #1 to safely fly down. by the time the fan restarted, employee #1 no longer had the strength to hang on, and let go, falling 45 ft to the wire netting below. employee #1 was hospitalized with contusions and abrasions.

a power line crew was assigned to work on a punch list for the overhead power lines for some rail tracks and to finish installing the switches on the catenary in a rail yard. a power line worker on the crew was working with a summer-help ground apprentice. when the crew started, tracks one and two were energized. at approximately 9:26 a.m., track two was deenergized. at approximately 12:21 p.m., track one was deenergized. tracks four and six had been deenergized though a long -term outage, and tracks one and two were to be deenergized until approximately 3:30 p.m. before tracks one and two were deenergized, the power line worker and his apprentice worked on tracks four and six. after tracks one and two were deenergized, he climbed the north side of the catenary and installed a switch for tracks one and two. he then connected a switch from track two to track four, completing this task at approximately 2:30 p.m. he next attached a 5.4-metric-ton hoist to the crossover between tracks two and four, and released the wings on the low-speed section insulator assembly between the contact wire and messenger wire. (the hoist was on the messenger line to release the load on the messenger cable so that the pin direction on the insulator could be changed. the pins were facing up with their bottoms, where the cotter pins were located, on top. the pins were to be inverted so that the cotter pins were at the bottom.) the employee had the grips and hoist in place on the messenger line. he installed a ground on the load side of the switch from track two to track four and tagged and tied it in the open position. the foreman notified the power line worker that power was be ing restored to tracks one and two. the foreman left and then returned and confirmed that tracks one and two were energized and that tracks four and six were still deenergized. working from an aerial lift, the power line worker grabbed the handle for the hoist. the hoist was energized, and the employee received an electric shock and suffered electrical burns. he was hospitalized for his injuries.

on march 9, 2006, employee #1 and a coworker were installing a new 15-ft-long fence post. employee #1 was holding the post upright, while the coworker was operating a front-end loader to pound the post into the ground. as the loader's bucket struck the top of the post, the bucket detached from the loader and fell on employee #1, crushing him and killing him. the approximate weight of the bucket was 2,000 pounds.

on july 3, 2002, employee #1, an excavation contractor, was using a backhoe to dig a trench at a new single-family home construction site. when the excavation reached the 12-ft depth, he climbed into the unprotected trench and used a probeto locate a 4-in. sewer pipe in order to make a connection. a portion of the excavation caved in, trapping employee #1. he called out to workers on another site , who summoned emergency responders. after extrication, employee #1 was taken to a hospital for treatment of bruises and a fractured knee.

at approximately 11:30 a.m. on may 22, 2000, employee #1 and another worker were in the process of placing sandbags on an air bubble that had developed on the plastic liner of an irrigation water reservoir. they were using a 12 ft aluminumboat to transport the sandbags to the air bubble located in the middle of the reservoir. when they reached the air bubble, one worker exited the boat and stoodon top of the air bubble to place the sandbags. employee #1 remained in the boatand handed over the sandbags. the boat started to take on water and sink slowly . employee #1 jumped off the boat into the water. he did not know how to swim an d was not wearing a life vest. employee #1 drowned.

employee #1 was removing material from an approximate 2-ft-wide by 6-to-7-ft-deep by 28-ft-long trench which was not shored nor was any type of protective shield in place. one trench wall was near vertical and the other wall was benched. a cave-in occurred which buried employee #1. rescuers entered the trench to dig employee #1 out when a second cave-in occurred striking them in the back. employee #1 suffered two broken wrists and was hospitalized.

an employee was working by assembly line number 2 stacking batteries onto a pallet. the employee stepped away from work area, and into the path of a forklift. the forklift ran over the right foot of the employee, which resulted in fractures and dislocations of bones in the right foot.

an employee positioned a step ladder against a wall to install an electronic device on a spray paint booth. as he stepped onto the top rung, the ladder slipped out from underneath him. the employee fell six feet to the concrete floor below. he was hospitalized for his injuries.
on march 2, 2002, employee #1's left foot was caught in a pinch point when a roll stop on the header roll wrap machine cycled down. employee #1 was hospitalized with a fractured foot.
on january 5, 2006, employee #1, a machine operator, was preparing to bend a piece of pipe when his fingers were crushed in a tube bending machine.
employee #1, a construction laborer, was knocked off from the area he was working, when a pipe clamp failed on a high pressure slurry pipeline.
on december 19, 2003, an employee hand was caught in a boiler machine running with its guard off. the employee was hospitalized and treated for burns.

at approximately 2:00 a.m. on july 24, 2011, employee #1 a maintenance worker, a nd employee #2 a coworker of earthgrains baking companies, inc. dba of sara leebakery, was working ovens #8, #2, #4. employee #1 lit 10 of the 44 burners using an electric high voltage lighting stick. employee #2 found employee #1 with his hand on the lighting stick shaking, and unable to release it. employee #2 pulle d the cord to the lighting stick and terminating the electrical source. employee #1 sustained electrical burns on his right hand and foot. employee #1 was hospitalized for his injuries. several factors caused the incident, such as, the employer did not provide a "lighting stick" which had any approvals, the "lighting stick" was not maintained free of recognized hazards and that the outer insulation had several breaks. the "lighting stick" had insulation that was not approved for electrical use, and did not provide mechanical protection equal to that of rubber equipment, and was not visually inspected for defects and damage prior to use.

at 7:00 a.m. on april 17, 1998, employee #1 was using a pee vee to straighten a log on the log transfer deck to the debarker. after several unsuccessful attempts to move the log into correct alignment the log contacted the inclined conveyoron one end which caused it to strike employee #1 in the chest knocking him back ward onto the debarker infeed conveyor. employee #1 was hospitalized with contus ions and abrasions to his chest and back.

employee #1 was cutting angled stock on a chop saw and attempted to clear and end-piece from under the guard that was equipped with side barriers. employee #1 reached in from the right side with his right hand and, while doing so, activated the foot activation pedal causing the saw blade to rise up and amputate the four fingers of the gloved right hand. employee #1 was hospitalized.
on august 9, 2001, an employee repairing an hvac unit contacted an energized conductor and was electrocuted.

on the morning of june 6, 1998, employee #1 was performing the duties of "material man" at central jersey concrete pipe company in farmingdale, nj. the company manufactures concrete pipes of varying diameters. as the material man, employee #1 was responsible for keeping the hoppers of raw materials (sand and gravel) filled. to do so, he was required to climb up (approximately 10 times a day) intoa shed-like structure in order to divert the material being fed from a conveyorabove, into the appropriate hopper. the shed was located at the top of the plant , and it contained the sand and gravel hoppers. for an unknown reason, employee #1, either fell or climbed into the sand hopper. while he was in there, sand was emptied from the bottom of the hopper, and as the sand collapsed around him he was engulfed and asphyxiated. by the time employee's #1 body was found, he had been dead for up to two hours, and his foot was protruding through the opening at the bottom of the hopper.

at approximately 9:45 p.m. on march 2, 1999, employee #1 was cleaning blender/mixer #4 in the meat grinding area. the employee worked for jmi sanitation, an out side contractor. while cleaning, the employee opened a door to remove left-over beef. the door served as an outlet on the blender/mixer. as he put his hand in the opening, his right index finger got caught in the moving auger (worm) and was amputated. employee #1 stated that he did not know whether the machine was running. he also stated that normally all the machines are shut down before cleaning begins.

at approximately 10:00 a.m. on november 4, 1999, employee #1 was using a modified polishing machine equipped with a mill-cutting disk to cut off the weld flash on motorcycle rims. the weld flash is a raised area of weld on the inside and outside of the rim where the rim is welded together. once the weld flash is remove d a belt sander is used to completely remove the weld. a coworker set the machine up and trained employee #1 on how the remove the weld flash. employee #1 had removed the weld flash on approximately fifteen rims when the accident occurred. employee #1 held the rim in his hands and pressed the rim against the horizontal polisher with the mill cutting disk. employee #1's right hand fingers were pres sing up on the bottom side of the rim and his left hand was holding and guiding the rim. the only thing keeping employee #1's hands of out of the dangerous point of operation was the rim. the work gloves that employee #1 was wearing becameentangled in the teeth of the mill cutting disk causing his right hand to be drawn into the point of operation. the coworker was preparing to polish some rims a nd was working about 10 ft behind employee #1 when the accident occurred. the coworker said he heard the rim drop, and then employee #1 ran over to him holding his right hand. the coworker noticed that employee #1's glove and fingers were still entangled in the mill cutting disk of the modified polisher. employee #1 was hospitalized for injuries that included: damaged tendons on the back of the right hand and right middle finger, amputation of the right ring finger and little finger. employee #1 was hospitalized.

at 9:00 a.m. on april 26, 2000, employee #1, of lamps plus in city of industry, ca was on a roof with a coworker, each carrying one end of a tarp full of roofing debris. employee #1 was facing west and walking between a row of 7 skylights a nd a single skylight in the middle of the roof when he tripped on the corner of the third skylight from the end of the roof. he fell head first and broke through the plastic skylight cover, then plummeted 30 ft onto the concrete floor. he was killed by the fall. the foreman was on the southeast corner of the roof and saw employee #1 just as he disappeared through the skylight, but did not see employee #1 trip. the skylight was unable to support employee #1's impact, broke. in apparent violation of title 8, california code of regulations, section 1632, the skylight openings were not guarded by standard railings and toeboards, nor were the covers capable of safely supporting the weight of a 200 lb person. the crew was working on the roof in areas where the skylight covers were not capable of supporting human weight. the skylight covers were plastic and had been cracked by the crews' hammering during the removal of roofing materials. crew members we re also closer than 25 in. to the skylights. all of these factors violated california osha regulations. the damaged and unguarded skylight covers led directly to the accident. an accident related serious violation of title 8, california code of regulations, section 1632 was to be issued. an associated general citation under title 8, california code of regulations, section 1509 (a) was also to be issued.

at approximately 7:00 a.m. on january 25, 2001, employee #1 was marking a manhole in the middle of an intersection. according to another employee, employee #1 was standing in the center of the street when a small white car driven by an unknown female driver hit him and dragged him approximately 40 feet. there were no barricades or red cones placed in middle of the road to detour traffic for the protection of employee #1. he was also not wearing an orange vest. the car was moving at a speed of approximately 30 miles per hour at the time of impact. employee #1 sustained a head injury and was transported to usc medical center where he was hospitalized for four days. .

at approximately 1:00 p.m. on june 13, 2003, an employee of northern californianail co. was working on the roof of a 40-foot warehouse nailing roof coverings with a nail gun. he was using a hitachi nail gun, with serial number c315813d. when his air hose got stuck on the roof, he tried to free the hose. in the process , the gun swung to the back of his head. he suffered a puncture wound on the back of his head when his right thumb accidentally tripped the trigger shooting a nail that was 2.125 inches long. the air pressure was approximately 110 psi. paramedics took him to saint francis hospital in lynwood, california, for medical treatment. the nail was removed from his head in the hospital, and he was released from the hospital after five days.

at approximately 9:51 a.m. on august 11, 2004, employee #1, a hvac service technician, was performing maintenance at a retail business. he was burned while welding an hvac unit atop a high ladder. he sustained minor burns to his right forearm and neck.

at approximately 10:47 p.m. on august 4, 2005, employee #1 was starting up a machine and feeding plastic film between a series of rolls. he was wearing gloves since the temperature of the plastic film was approximately 280-300 degrees f. the process required an employee to squat underneath the machine and another employee to grasp the film after it passed between the rolls. that employee would the n tape the plastic film to the roll using duct tape to hold it in place as it passed between another set of rolls. after the product is fed through, a button is pressed to close the gap between rolls and the material then becomes embossed. on the day of the accident, employee #1 was manually feeding the plastic film be tween the rubber roll and the embossing roll when his right hand became caught between the rolls. he was hospitalized with his injury. the gap between the rolls was approximately 1.5 inches.

at approximately 5:55 p.m. on january 23, 2009, employee #1, a security guard, was found unconscious under a fallen gate located at the diamond head side of a commercial property. employee #1 worked alone at this site. a bystander called 91 1 at around 5:56 p.m. ems arrived at the site at 6:04 p.m. and then transported employee #1 to queen's medical center, where he was pronounced dead two days later.

employee #1 was struck by the magic carpet or ali baba amusement ride. employee #1 was standing on the inside of the ride and evidently saw a child climb on the outside of the ride enclosure and went to get the child down when he was struck by the passenger car of the ride. employee #1 was hospitalized with a concussion.

employee #1 was working as a temporary employee at sst bearings corporation, operating the swedger horizonal hydraulic press in the conveyor roller department. he reached into the press with his right hand while holding a part, and depresse d the start button with his left hand. the press was activated and it amputated his right index and middle fingers, above the first knuckle. he was hospitalized .

on january 29, 2004, employee #1 was delivering residential trim to a job site. while he entered the residence, yelling for help to unload the trim, he opened a door to what he thought was a closet. his intent was to check the quality of the trim work he milled. employee #1 stepped in the opening that the door had beenblocking and fell 10 feet down an elevator shaft. he was hospitalized ant treated for injuries sustained.

on march 5, 2007, employee #1, a sole proprietor owner of a remodeling company, was installing drywall on the interior of a room that was 13.3 ft in height. employee #1 was preparing an outside corner for a corner bead molding and then joint compound. employee #1 was working from a 10-ft high aluminum, 1a, stepladder when he fell; landing first on his feet, and then his head struck the linoleum tile floor. employee #1 was taken to a local hospital and, on march 15, 2007, he passed away. the county coroner's office stated that his death was from a blunt force trauma to the head.
on december 17, 2004, employee #1, a machine operator, was electrocuted while working on a piece of equipment. employee #1 was killed.

two employees were replacing a 4-in. gate valve on a return line for a boiler system. employee #1 was approximately 4 ft up on a ladder. employee #2 was on an elevated platform on the other side of the piping. while working, employee #1 struck the back of his head on the piping that ran behind him while he was on the ladder. employee #2 removed him from the ladder and summoned help. employee #1 was taken to the hospital and died two days later from severe head trauma.
on september 13, 2005, employee #1 collapsed while unloading wood pellets. the employee died from heat stroke.
on january 13, 2003, an employee was found slumped over, sitting in the seat of a forklift truck. information provided to the toledo area office indicated thatthe employee died from a heart attack.

on november 13, 2006, employee #1 was cleaning a doa machine type d1, serial number 512-19784. it was standard procedure to clean the machine while it was in operation. employee #1 reached in to remove the lint and fuzz from the area around the gears and his hand got caught in the gears. employee #1's right hand was crushed and required amputation. employee #1 was hospitalized.

at approximately 11:00 a.m. on april 16, 2007, employee #1 was standing at his work station at a cnc lumber cutting machine waiting for a job to change over. a few minutes later, the employee was discovered lying on the concrete floor by a coworker. the floor was dry and lightly coated with fine saw dust. no witnesses observed the employee falling to the floor or what precipitated the fall. employee was transported to the mercer county community hospital, coldwater, ohio, and subsequently transported by helicopter to miami valley hospital, dayton, ohio. employee #1 died at 1:00 a.m. on april 17, 2007. cororner's report indicated that the death was due to blunt force trauma to the head.

at 6:30 p.m. on january 15, 2008, employee #1, and a coworker, were cleaning cobwebs from a building's interior horizontal walls. employee #1 and the coworker were using standard push brooms equipped with extensions. inside was an interiorbuilding that housed the building's sprinkler system. for some reason, employee #1 climbed up the interior building to stand on the roof. employee #1 did not see the roof edge and fell 10 ft to the floor. employee #1 sustained a fractured wrist. employee #1 was not hospitalized.

at approximately 11:30 a.m. on august 26, 2008, an employee and a coworker were servicing the machine while the strapping or head unit of m.j. maillis strapping system, serial number 4875-1-0608, was in a raised position on the track. the s

at approximately 12:00 a.m. on march 10, 2009, employee #1 was operating a sankiseiko stk-2622-150 hydraulic press machine that was designed by the company as ich must be depressed with one foot and is guarded with light curtains on the front of the machine where the operator accesses the press area and on the right hand side of the press. the left hand side of the press was guarded with a standard interlocked barrier guard with the back of the press unguarded but was inacce ssible by employees. employee #1 had just placed a large plastic part into the press, and was removing a piece of scrap from the press that was between the light curtain and the point of operation. employee #1 reached into the hydraulic plastic trim press and the press cycle was inadvertently initiated. employee #1 sustained severe crushing injuries to the upper body. two of employee #1's coworker s were nearby, at the time of the accident. one coworker was approximately 25 ftaway, waiting for a part with their back to the press. a second coworker was approximately 10 ft away, entering data on a computer terminal, directly adjacent to the press operating controls and foot activation pedal, with their left side facing the press. employee #1 died as a result of the accident.
an employee was working on a roof at an apartment complex when he raised a piece of copper tubing and made contact with an overhead power line. the employee died in a burn center 4 weeks later.

at approximately 3:20 p.m. on july 23, 2012, employee #1 a cement mixer was driving a cement mixer truck. the employee was going under a lirr overpass on oyster bay road in locust valley. the truck was too high to clear the overpass. the truck went out of control and collided head-on with a school bus traveling on the same road. the driver of school bus died at the scene. four children and matron that were aboard the bus were injured. employee #1 and the others that were injured were taken to an area hospital, where they were treated for unspecified fractures. employee #1 remains hospitalized.
