at 4:20 p.m. on december 13, 2021, an employee was replacing a hydraulic cylinder in a blow molding machine when the cylinder shifted while his finger was through the clevis. the employee suffered an amputation to the tip of his left middle finger and was treated without hospitalization.

an employee was working for a contractor that supported gas and oil operations. on or before december 21, 2021, a leak was found on a flange for a bypass line on the hydro desulfurization unit (hdu-1) at refinery operated by an international oil company. the leak was detected because the leak had auto ignited. the refinery owner's fire team had been called out to put the fire out. after the fire was out, steam lances were continuously directed on the flange due to the potential of another flash fire from the leaking heated naphtha. the refinery's owner hired two contract companies to repair the leaking flange using a method called hot bolting. in this method, one bolt at a time was removed and replaced so that workers could install a wire wrap and seal the leak. the replacement bolts were longer and had injection collars. workers would do the wire wrap and inject the bolts with a sealant to stop the leak. at 1:00 a.m. on december 23, 2021, an employee was working for the contracting firm that would remove and replace the bolts, while the other contractor assisted. as the employee was waiting for the other contractor, an explosion occurred during the repair. the employee suffered second-degree burns. in addition, he suffered a fractured femur when he was struck by a flying object from the explosion. he was hospitalized. another worker there was not hospitalized. the employee's supervisors knew that the flange had auto ignited the day before they were called to come and fix the leak. the supervisors did not, though, ensure that employees had upgraded their personal protective equipment (ppe) to reduce the risk of serious injuries should a flash fire reoccur. they did not evaluate the hazards and select and require the use of appropriate ppe using their knowledgeof the operating conditions, nor did they use refinery owner's safe work practice requirement of using upgraded ppe when work activity is going to be done in areas with potential flash fire.

at 2:23 a.m. on july 11, 2021, an employee was working as a truck driver for a logistics delivery and consulting service for restaurants. the employee was making a delivery to a client. the employee was pulling products in trays onto the truck liftgate of a trailer when he fell backwards approximately 4 feet ontothe ground. the employee had been using a metal hook to pull a tray when he lost his grip due to wearing wet gloves. the employee was working alone at the time. he called his employer and they called for paramedics. the employee was transported to the hospital, where he was admitted with a punctured lung and head injury.
at 6:45 a.m. on december 18, 2021, an employee reported to work and shot herselfin the head with a gun, i.e., suicide.

at 5:15 a.m. on january 9, 2022, an employee was cleaning the area around a cotton gin lint cleaner when a fire started in a cotton bail causing the facility alarms to sound. the company shut down all of the cotton gins to check the area for any ignition sources or smoke. it takes approximately five minutes for the machines to completely shut down. the employee entered an area that was blocked off by chains and locked. the employee thought the gin had been locked and tagged out when he reached in to clean out the clogged machine. the employeewas hospitalized to treat an amputation of his left hand.

at 3:00 p.m. on may 13, 2020, an employee came into the shop and started his shift.  the employer is engaged in auto body manufacturing fabrication and painting operations.  the employee was hired as a helper, who routinely performed the task of helping the operation of cutting and bending metals and sometimes helping the painters. the employee started his shift working at the cutting area for 2 to 3 hours, then was transferred to the bending area.  the employee was using a press brake machine (model 1214r). the employee sustained a crush injury to his left hand and finger, causing an amputation of his fingers. the employee was hospitalized for three days.

at 11: 30 a.m. on december 27, 2021, an employee was working for a temporary staffing employment agency. he had been sent to work at a general, local freighttrucking company. at their site, the employee was operating a forklift. he was offloading palletized merchandise from a truck's container. the container and the loading dock where the employee was working were 4.25 feet (1.3 meters) above the ground. the truck driver, assuming the offloading process was complete, began to drive away. the forklift, with the employee still in the forklift cab, rolled in reverse and fell out from the truck container onto the ground. he fell approximately 4 feet (1.2 meters). the employee suffered lower back pain. specifically, he suffered muscle aches and contusions in his back and shoulder. he would need to undergo surgery. he was not hospitalized immediately after the incident.

at 8:00 a.m. on october 12, 2021, an employee was changing blades and making adjustments on a powermatic, model number 66, serial number 91661419 table saw in a wood shop area of an air filter manufacturing facility. the employee was changing the blades from an aluminum cutting blade to one for masonite engineered wood. when the employee started adjusting the fence of the table saw, he accidentally contacted the running blade and suffered a laceration to the thumb of his left hand. the employee required medical treatment but was not hospitalized.

at 2:30 p.m. on january 24, 2022, an employee used a table saw to cut wood. while making a cut, the saw kicked back, and the employee's hand contacted the blade at the right index finger. the employee was hospitalized to treat an amputation of the finger.

at 12:52 p.m. on august 7, 2021, a fire crew was completing a fire line on a side hill at a remote location on the dixie incident near chester, ca. while redirecting the crew around a fallen log, an approximately 100+ foot tall tree fell, striking and injuring four firefighters. employee #1 was hospitalized with head and facial lacerations, back and vertebrae fractures, rib fractures, second- to third-degree burns, and a shoulder injury. employee #2 was treated for three broken ribs. employee #3 was treated for a knee injury and possible concussion. employee #4 was treated for a forearm contusion.  employees #2, #3, and #4 were not hospitalized.

at 10:00 a.m. on january 5, 2022, an employee was instructed by his supervisor to move away from the bobcat conducting push/dragging operations and stand in a safe location until the supervisor could talk to him. the employee had started to move to a designated location as the supervisor continued his work. the employee was struck by the bobcat.  the employee was hospitalized to treat fractures to his legs.

at approximately 9:00 a.m. on february 11, 2022, employee #1 and employee #2 were in the process of unloading a sea container of birch vanier plywood.  the employees went to physically tip the plywood bundle when the sheets broke loose from the strapping and fell onto them. employee# 1 suffered a severe leg sprain. employee# 2 suffered a broken bone in his chest area.  the employees were hospitalized to treat their injuries.

at 1:00 p.m. on october 15, 2021, an employee was working on the power take-off (pto) shaft of a tank wagon truck. the employee climbed under the running vehicle to work on the pto shaft and was caught by the spinning shaft, pulling him in by his clothing. the employee was killed by mechanical asphyxiation.

on december 24, 2021, an employee tested positive for covid-19 but did not require hospitalization. on december 27, 2021, after the initial positive case was reported, multiple coworkers tested positive. the building was shut down for cleaning and testing.  after the initial positive case of covid-19, additional coworkers tested positive on december 27, 2021.

on august 18, 2020, an employee was making deliveries in his non-air-conditioned delivery truck. he began his shift at approximately 8:30 a.m. he started feeling unwell and notified the employer using his hand-held device. when the employee did not respond to the employer's messages, the employer requested a welfare check from the police department using his gps location. paramedics were called by the police department, and the employee was transported to san antonio regional hospital and hospitalized to treat acute kidney injury due to heat-related illness.

at 11:10 a.m. on march 16, 2022, an employee was in the process of changing a ballast for a light on a paint booth.  the employee came into contact with an electrical source and was killed by electrocution.

at 11:00 a.m. on december 6. 2021, an employee was helping a coworker who was attempting to unjam a watkins aircraft supports products chute while setting up loading equipment at a truck trailer. the coworker had the employee help push the chute when he caught his left hand in the chute flap. the employee amputated his index finger, requiring medical treatment without hospitalization.
treated without  hospitalization.

at 11:15 p.m. on march 27, 2022, an employee started his shift as a new employee.  he walked up multiple flights of stairs to access a coker unit platform level. the employee collapsed when he reached the platform surface. the employee died due to natural causes, i.e., an apparent heart attack.

at 5:00 p.m. on march 23, 2022, an employee was attempting to elevate a trailer to hook to a truck. the employee was caught between the trailer (drake trailer) and the truck tires (make - 2020 western star; model - conventional 4). the employee's chest was crushed, and he was killed.

at 11:50 a.m. on february 24, 2022, an employee was working as the plant electrician for a firm that produced roasted nuts and made almond butter. he was working with electrical equipment in the butter/grinding room. the employer was installing new equipment for this operation, including an a.c. horn milling machine, with serial number sn33 988r. the firm's engineering team had asked the employee to determine why the ism laser management tool on top of the machine'shopper was providing incorrect readings. the laser was attached to a bung welded to the hopper. the employee saw that he had to bend the bung, as it was not level. he borrowed a 30 inch (760 mm) pry bar from a coworker and climbed a 6 foot (1.8 meter) ladder up to the fourth rung, or approximately 4 feet (1.2 meters). the layout of the equipment would not let the employee position the ladder perpendicular to the equipment and let him get closer to the sensor, so he placed the ladder parallel to the equipment. at this angle, he had to twist to his right to place the pry bar under the bung. a video of the incident showed that he had both hands on the pry bar, applying a lot of force. as he was pulling down on the pry bar, it slipped. he fell approximately 6 feet (1.8 meters) from the ladder or machinery to the floor. he initially landed on his feet but fell to the floor and finally onto his right side. he suffered a compression fracture of his l4 vertebra and was hospitalized.  he had experienced prior issues with his back. the employer claimed that due to the tight quarters, there was no other way to safely access the bung and sensor. during an inspection, the csho noticed a scissor lift next to the equipment. a subcontractor doing electrical work on the equipment had brought it in. after the incident, the employer completed a job hazard analysis for the sensor replacement, as required by their written safety program. the use of a scissor lift was made a requirement for changing out the sensor.

at 10:48 a.m. on april 8, 2022, an employee walked behind a tracked excavator to talk to a coworker, who was operating the excavator. the coworker completed his task of unloading electric motors from a metal hopper with the excavator. the coworker turned the cab around (swing) and started moving forward, running over the employee with one of the tracks. the employee was killed.

at 10:40 a.m. on october 21, 2021, six employees were working for a staffing agency that provided farm workers for agricultural sites. they were not migrant workers. they were packing grapes at a grape vineyard. at approximately 10:00 a.m., an employee was in the break area located outside the grape packing area outside the building when she had a medical emergency for a reported preexisting medical condition. she was transported to a medical clinic by her supervisor. while the supervisor was offsite, other employees in the indoor packing room and outdoor break area outside the packing room complained of an unknown odor. employees of the staffing agency called emergency medical services. four local agencies responded. the building was evacuated by the county fire department. the building was cleared by a hazardous material agency after an interior entry and inspection by the hazardous material team. it was determined that the grapepacking room was cooled by a mixture of propylene glycol and water. no abnormal air readings were found at the time of the incident by the facility plant manager or first responders. no evidence of a refrigeration leak was found in the grape packing area or from other adjacent refrigerated areas of the facility the building was found to be safe and returned back to control of the vineyard at approximately 4:00 p.m. packing operations resumed the next day without incident. no chemical release was found. the workers had experienced chest pain, headache, and nausea. they were all treated witout hospitalization. this was a joint employer site, but it was not a multiemployer site. this incident is alsorecorded as investigation number 145337, which covers the staffing agency. thatinvestigation lists seven employees. the upa is the same for both, 1829092.

at 2:45 p.m. on december 27, 2021, an employee worked to install a six-foot section of sheeting between a window and the corner of a building. the employeeclimbed a sixteen-foot extension ladder to reach the location of the work, which was placed on slippery and muddy ground. the employee stood on the top rung to nail in a twelve-inch by eight-foot strip of wood in place. when the employee began to work, the ladder shifted approximately an inch, which caused the employee to look down and see that the ladder was starting to kick outward. the employee attempted to grab ahold of the nearest window, missed, and fell approximately twelve feet, and struck a fire sprinkler standpipe on the way down. the employee asked to go home and shower, feeling stiffness after the fall. the employee then went to the hospital the next day and was treated for multiple contusions and abrasions to the lower back and buttocks.
shortly after opening the store, an armed robber entered the store and shot employee #1, killing him.

at 2:00 p.m. on june 1, 2022, an employee was riding on the back of a landscapetrailer.  while on the ground, the employee was run over by the truck/trailer. the employee sustained multiple unspecified crushing injuries and was killed.
at 12:06 p.m. on august 23, 2022, an employee was planting trees when he suddenly collapsed. the employee was killed due to possible natural causes. the incident occurred on tribal lands.

at 10:00 a.m. on october 5, 2022, an employee was working with coworkers conducting site clearing work. the employee was assigned to pull roots off a nearby building pad and remove silt fencing from the northside of the site nearthe tree removal operation. a coworker was assigned to operate a hitachi zaxis 300lc excavator to fell trees from the northside of the site. one tree the coworker had felled was an approximately 81-foot-tall tree. during the removal operations, the coworker checked his surroundings, dug around the tree, and pulled the tree out of the ground using the excavator bucket with welded thumb attachment. the coworker yarded the tree to the landing near the gc trailer towards the exit of the jobsite using the same excavator. on the way back to the tree removal operation, the coworker noticed the employee next to a 6-foot dirt pile approximately 15 feet away from him. before felling the next tree out of the ground, the coworker conducted the normal routine check to make sure no person was in the area. the equipment operator went to fell the tree out of the ground. with the tree already in motion, the coworker saw the employee standing in the area the tree was free falling. the employee was standing near two dirt piles, approximately 6-foot high on the side of the excavator operator. after noticing the employee so close to the tree removal operation, the coworker attempted to stop the excavator bucket and reverse the motion away from the employee. since the tree was so large and the employee was so close, the tree struck the employee in the head causing blunt force trauma and he died. the employee was transported to a medical center for treatment but died later that day.

at 1:15 p.m. on october 28, 2022, an employee was cutting a tree on a logging site.  the cut tree became entangled with another tree and broke that tree in two places.  when the second section broke, it bent back toward the employee that cut the tree, striking him in the head. the employee was killed due to headtrauma.

at 12: 00 p.m. on june 17, 2022, employee #1 had finished the work shift, breaking down and loading equipment onto trailers in preparation for travel to the next carnival stop. travel to the next stop was delayed, so employee #1 went to take a nap in his makeshift bunkhouse located inside a travel trailer that housed a large generator and other equipment. later, coworker #1 knocked on the bunkhouse door where employee #1 was napping inside of the trailer and received no response.  coworker #1 noticed that there was a smaller generator stored inside of the travel trailer, but thought nothing of it, as it was not running. at approximately 4:30 p.m., coworkers #1 and #2 entered the trailer to knock on the door to employee #1's bunkhouse and heard a whimpering sound from inside. the coworkers began working together to break the bunkhouse door down and found employee #1 and his dog inside the bunkhouse room unconscious. 911 was called. at 4:53 p.m., emergency personnel arrived, but attempts to resuscitate employee #1 failed. at 5:04 p.m., employee #1 was pronounced dead.   due to generators being stored near employee #1's bunkhouse, the fire department obtained carbon monoxide (co) measurements. at 5:08 p.m., a level of 83 parts per million (ppm) co was measured, and co levels did not reach 0 ppm until 5:50 p.m. the medical examiner performed an autopsy revealing employee #1's co blood saturation levelto be at a fatal level of 60%. the dog was taken to a local emergency veterinarian and also died. the cause of death was from co exposure from the use of the small generator inside of the trailer to power electric equipment inside of employee #1's bunkhouse, including but not limited to an air conditioner, a television, and a refrigerator. the larger generator is not thought to have beena contributing factor as its exhaust was vented to the outside of the trailer.

at 9:40 a.m. on december 9, 2022, an employee who performed maintenance duties was inspecting the sag in a crane cable when he was struck by the crane's rear catenary trolley. the employee incurred multiple blunt force trauma injuries to the back and neck and was killed.

at 10:00 a.m. on february 7, 2023, an employee was found unresponsive in the control atmospheric (ca) room.  the typical oxygen level when the room is active is between 1.2% to 1.6%.  the room has a main door and a smaller man-door; the man-door was open and the employee was right inside. the employee was killed dueto oxygen deficiency.

at 11:07 p.m. on march 30, 2023, an employee working as a delivery driver for a restaurant was delivering a pizza. the employee was shot in the chest by an unknown assailant. the gunshot went through the employee's chest out his back, left to right and at a downward angle. the employee died as a result of his injuries.
at 12:30 a.m. on may 5, 2023, an employee was conducting a sales transaction. two armed assailants entered and began to open fire.  the employee was killed due to a gunshot wound.

at 7:52 a.m. on may 16, 2023, an employee was inside a facility shop, remodeling a cougar by keysone recreational fifth wheel camper (serial number: 019-000455732), which involved a full tear out of the interior and front wall of the trailer. the employer is engaged in the construction of system engineered buildings for farm and industrial purposes, this work was not a regular process conducted by this employer. the employee and a coworker were replacing the ceiling on the front side of the trailer. the employee was using a keller 4 fiberglass stepladder (type 1, model illegible) to replace the roof plywood. the employee fell, unwitnessed, less than 6 feet from the open end of the camper, striking the concrete floor. the employee suffered a head laceration and fractures behind his left eye and on the lower back of his neck and back. the coworker attempted first aid until the ambulance arrived and transported the employee to a local trauma center.  the employee suffered a massive stroke and was placed on life support at the hospital, he died two days later.

at 10:22 p.m. on august 28, 2023, an employee was undergoing training for loading tractor trailers.  while talking to a coworker, the employee collapsed against a wall and became unresponsive.  the employee died from an unknown cause.

at 12:00 a.m. on august 20, 2023, an employee was performing normal work tasks. the employee contracted legionnaires' disease from a contaminated water source. the employee died as a result of legionnaires' disease.

at 6:00 p.m. on december 4, 2023, an employee working as a housekeeper for a janitorial services company was working in a large hotel. the employee collapsed in the hallway of the hotel during her shift while performing housekeeping work. the death certificate stated that employee died from sudden cardiac arrest.

at 5:30 p.m. on september 4, 2023, an employee was watching a private tree feller and assisting the tree feller with packing jacks. the tree feller was using jacks to fell a seven-foot-wide, 200-foot-tall redwood tree uphill. the tree jack slipped out of the tree, and the tree began to fall. the tree feller ran to safety, and the employee ran to the tree to get the jack. the employee was struck by the falling tree and was killed.
at 9:00 a.m. on january 30, 2024, an employee was stacking lumber, when his safety vest got caught in the chain machine.  the employee was killed.

at 4:30 p.m. on february 4, 2024, an employee working for an auto parts store was delivering parts to a customer site. once he arrived onsite, the employee entered a garage door. the employee was walking up a ramp that had a trailer on it when he fell back off the ramp and hit his head on the concrete floor. the employee died from multiple skull fractures and brain bleeds.

at 2:46 p.m. on december 14, 2023, an employee had disposed of cardboard boxes in a trash bin. when returning back to his workstation, the employee slipped on a dropped piece of cardboard and went headfirst into a wooden support pole. the employee appeared to be intoxicated. the employee was killed due to blunt force trauma to the forehead.

at 12:15 a.m. on march 7, 2024, employees were lifting a piece of heavy equipment onto a lowboy with a digger derrick, when either the main lift rope or the rigging failed, crushing and killing one of the employees.
at 10:00 a.m. on april 25, 2024, an employee was taking tags off of bales of cotton. the employee was crushed and killed between the bales of cotton.

at 8:15 a.m. on may 18, 2024, an employee was conducting a u-turn with a crane, preparing for relocation to another jobsite. while in a drive configuration, the right rear tire of the crane encountered a soft shoulder of the driveway. the shoulder collapsed under the weight of the crane, causing it to roll down a hill. the employee initially jumped free from the crane when the shoulder collapsed, but reentered the crane to set the parking brake. at this time, the crane rolled over, and the employee was caught between the ground and the crane's wheel hub, resulting in fatal injuries.

at 10:30 p.m. on january 15, 2024, an employee working as a truck driver for a freight service was unloading cars from the top deck of a cottrell 7509 car hauler connected to a 2013 peterbilt 389 truck. while the employee was in the process of unloading the last car, he fell from the top deck of a car hauler and struck the pavement. the employee died from multiple injuries.

at 3:15 p.m. on june 1, 2024, an employee working as an iron worker was working with two coworkers, also, iron workers, replacing the end trucks on an overheadcrane. no production work was ongoing in the area at the time. after the two coworkers had replaced the end trucks, the employee was told that the drive motors on the east and west sides of the crane were running in opposite directions. the employee then worked to troubleshoot and check the phasing of the drive motors using a jlg model 2630 es scissor lift (serial #: d200137108) to access the crane. the employee completed the work so the drive motors would run in the same direction. the crane was not de-energized or locked out at this time. the employee attempted to test the crane which appeared to run appropriately in the southern direction. but when he tested in the northern direction, it appeared to not stop and struck the scissor lift tipping it over. the employee was in control of the crane at this time. the employee fell approximately 17 feet with the lift and struck the concrete ground below, possibly also striking one or more worktables. the employee sustained head, back, arm and abdominal injuries and died from a traumatic brain injury (tbi).

at 3:00 p.m. on june 14, 2024, an employee working for a remediation service had finished dumping debris at a landfill. the employee was leaving the landfill property and stopped his dump truck right outside the entrance to check on the truck brakes. he attempted to reconnect a brake airline when the truck moved forward and ran over the employee. the employee sustained multiple crush injuries to his left side. the employee died as a result injuries to his hip, arm and chest.

at 1:00 p.m. on june 24, 2024, an employee and coworker #1 were working as electricians for an electrical contractor. the team was installing lighting switches and other associated electrical equipment in room 716 of a commercial building. the team deenergized a circuit that supplied power to an area where they needed to perform work. they thought they had isolated the live circuit from the circuit that fed the area where they needed to work. coworker #1 conducted some testing on a switch he was working on which was supposed to be deenergized. he discovered through testing the circuit with a hot stick that it was still energized. coworker #1 then tested the switch with his voltage meter, which indicated a 0-voltage reading. he was not sure why the hot stick was indicating a voltage reading and how the power was getting to the switch. coworker #1 was in charge of the site because the master electrician was not on site, so he instructed the employee to go inside room 716 and determine whether the junction box they installed earlier in the day was indicating the same issuehe had observed at the switch. after coworker #1 instructed the employee to do the testing in the junction box, he went down to the third floor to use the bathroom. the employee went into room 716 to conduct testing on the junction box. coworker #2 was in the room adjacent to room 716. coworker #2 heard the employee yell, so he went to see what had occurred and saw the employee standing on a ladder with his upper body above the suspended ceiling. coworker #2 attempted to initiate a conversation with the employee, but the employee was unresponsive. coworker #2 then contacted coworker #1 via phone to notify him of the situation. coworker #1 returned to room 716 to find the employee on the floor and called 911. the employee had somehow contacted a live electrical circuit and died due to being electrocuted.

at 11:42 a.m. on may 31, 2024, employee #1 was trimming a tree and moving from one branch to another. while relocating, the employee failed to reconnect his fall protection gear and fell from the tree. the employee sustained a head injury and was later pronounced dead at the scene.
july 16, 2024, the employee died from her injuries including fractures to the left and right sides of her skull, a brain bleed and a stroke.

at 11:00 a.m. on october 21, 2024, employee #1 was removing latches from the lidof a semi-trailer while standing on top of it, using a saferack gangway with a guard rail system. the employee was pulled by the gangway and guard rail systemand fell approximately 13 feet 6 inches to the ground. employee #1 sustained fatal injuries.

at 9:30 p.m. on november 4, 2024, an employee was working from the basket of a genie s-40 telescopic boom lift, preparing to hang tarps to paint the underside of an overpass on westbound i-90/94 near exit 50-b. a tractor-trailer struck the basket of the aerial lift, causing the employee to be thrown against a nearby structural steel beam. the employee sustained blunt force injuries and was transported to a hospital, where he was declared dead.

at 4:10 p.m. on november 25, 2024, an employee working at a slaughterhouse was working on a schedule that allowed him to acclimate to the environment. the employee was attempting to qualify to the 80 percent work and 20 percent rest cycle (80/20), but was still working at a 60/40 cycle. the employee's position required him to remove the butt hide section of a carcass using an air knife. the employee had completed two 15-minute rotations and was currently resting for 5 minutes. the employee signaled a coworker (the supervisor) that he was ready to resume work. about a minute later, the coworker handed the employee the knife. the employee took one step and collapsed into his supervisor's arms. the employee went into cardiac arrest and possibly into defibrillation. the staff nurses onsite used an available aed to attempt to save the employee. ems arrived approximately 20 minutes later and took over. the employee died from an unspecifiied cardiac event.

at 1:27 p.m. on january 2, 2025, employee #1, a 48-year-old male who had been employed for two days at a cannabis production facility, was working in the sorting room when he suddenly experienced chest pain and collapsed to the floor. the employer immediately activated its emergency action plan, called 911, and retrieved an aed. while following instructions from the 911 operator, cpr was administered until emergency medical services arrived. despite life-saving efforts, the employee was pronounced deceased. the employee had a known preexisting medical condition.

at 4:15 p.m. on february 10, 2025, employee #1 was working for a logging company operating a semi-truck attached to a trailer. the trailer was loaded with logs descending downhill when employee #1 reportedly lost control causing the truck to flip over. the cab of the truck  overturned and  employee #1 was trapped. employee #1 sustained fatal injuries.

at 12:00 p.m. on january 3, 2025, employee #1 and coworkers had removed a largesection of a metal roof to repair support structures, add insulation, and install new roofing material. after helping add a channel to the structure, employee #1 climbed on the roof without any fall protection. the employee fell approximately 20 feet through the open section of the roof, sustaining fatal injuries.
on june 18, 2001, employee #1 fell from a ladder and sustained fatal head injuries.

an apprentice electrician was working atop a 12.6-kilovolt (7.2-kilovolt-to-ground) three-phase switchgear that was 2.7 meters high. he was working near the service entry point, attempting to maneuver a 76-millimeter-diameter flexible metal conduct (ac-corflex) into a knockout opening at the raceway interface with the switchgear. the supply conductors for the switchgear were energized and were 1.2 meters from the raceway. (the plant had a scheduled shutdown for a 3-hour period later that day, when the service to the switchgear could have been deenergized .) the employee contacted one of the supply phase conductors and was electrocute d.
